file.content:
# Petitioner Registration — Contract

Status: **Draft**

> **⚠️ v0 scope.** This document fixes the *registration-side* contract
> between the (forthcoming) petition-scheduler Reckoner and the plugins
> that emit petitions to it. v0 covers the registration call shape, the
> source-id registry, the handle-rooted emit and withdraw surface, the
> three opt-in feedback channels, the authority-to-priority gate, and
> the v0 stance on lifecycle hooks (none) and shared base classes
> (none — recipes only). The intake-side validators, the Reckonings
> book schema, the patron-emit surface (CLI / MCP), the renamed
> queue-observer / pulse-emitter, and any non-`patron` / non-`vision-keeper`
> petitioner are explicitly out of scope. See [Open Questions](#open-questions)
> for the named follow-ups.

---

## Dependencies

```
contract spans: ['clerk', 'clockworks', 'lattice', 'spider']
```

- **The [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md)** — the closest live precedent for a
  runtime-laden, single-surface, programmatic registry sealed at
  `phase:started`. The petitioner registry mirrors Clerk's writ-type
  registry almost verbatim (registration call shape, duplicate check,
  fail-loud-on-unknown stance, self-registration of the framework's
  one mandatory entry).
- **[Clockworks](clockworks.md)** — the event-and-CDC substrate the
  feedback-receipt section routes through. Standing orders and the
  auto-wired `book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` events
  are the only sanctioned petitioner-feedback path in v0.
- **The [Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md)** — the source of the
  `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source-id grammar this contract adopts,
  and the precedent the doc's identity section explicitly diverges
  from (Lattice trusts emitter-stamped `pulse.source` strings; this
  contract returns a typed handle and stamps the source internally).
- **The [Spider](apparatus/spider.md)** — the source of the
  framework-wide kit-vs-kit collision policy this contract adopts:
  a duplicate registration with the same source string is a hard
  startup error that names both contributing plugins.

---

## 1. Registration mechanism

A plugin declares itself a petitioner by calling `registerPetitioner` on
the Reckoner's runtime API from inside its own apparatus's `start()`:

```typescript
const handle: PetitionerHandle = reckoner.registerPetitioner({
  source: 'vision-keeper.snapshot',
  allowedPriorities: ['urgent', 'normal', 'low'],
  description: 'Periodic vision-vs-reality snapshots emitted when '
             + 'the keeper observes drift worth surfacing.',
});
```

Registration is **programmatic only**. There is no `petitioners` field
on a kit export, no `guild.json` registry of declared sources, and no
declarative side-channel that completes the binding without a runtime
call. `registerPetitioner` is the single-surface entry point — the
same trade-off the Clerk made for writ types (per `c-mod9a2gh`):
petitioners are runtime-laden (the registration call returns a handle
that closes over the source string and the allowed-priority allow-list
and that is the only path through which `emit` and `withdraw` can be
reached), and a static kit channel cannot complete the runtime-binding
step on its own. Co-locating registration with the apparatus's
runtime state keeps the binding mechanical and the call site grep-
findable.

Each `registerPetitioner` call accepts the static descriptor below
(D8 — no hooks in v0; D7 — `allowedPriorities` is the authority gate's
canonical home):

```typescript
interface RegisterPetitionerRequest {
  /** Fully-qualified source id. See §2 for the grammar. */
  source: string;
  /**
   * Priority levels this source may emit. Optional; omitting yields
   * the default `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`. See §7 for the gate.
   */
  allowedPriorities?: PetitionPriority[];
  /** Short human-facing description for diagnostics and Oculus. */
  description: string;
}
```

No optional `onAccept` / `canRetry` / `onDefer` hooks are accepted in
v0. The deferred hook surface is named in [Open Questions](#open-questions);
the rationale is that the upstream conclusion (per `c-mod9a48y`)
already settled "no direct callback mechanism in v0," and the
observation need is covered by the CDC + standing-order recipes in
§4 without an additional invocation-ordering contract.

### Registry seal

content.content:
100	
101	The petitioner registry seals at the framework's global
102	`phase:started` signal — the moment every apparatus's `start()` has
103	finished. After the seal, every `registerPetitioner` call throws
104	with a clear `[reckoner] registerPetitioner: cannot register
105	petitioner "<source>" — the startup registration window has closed.`
106	diagnostic, naming the offending source. This matches the writ-type
107	seal in the [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry): the
108	"apparatus startup is the registration window" invariant is framework-
109	wide, not Reckoner-specific. By the time the first `crawl()` /
110	scheduler tick runs, the registry is inspectable and immutable.
111	
112	### Kit-vs-kit collision policy
113	
114	Two `registerPetitioner` calls with the same `source` string are a
115	**hard error**. The second call throws at registration time with a
116	diagnostic that names *both* contributing plugin ids and the
117	conflicting source — the winner is never selected by load order. This
118	mirrors the framework-wide rule the Spider documents for
119	`rigTemplateMappings` (per `c-mod9a6x3`'s lineage) and that
120	[Clerk applies to writ-types](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry),
121	[Spider to `blockTypes` / `rigTemplateMappings`](apparatus/spider.md#plugin-default-template-and-mapping),
122	and the Fabricator to engine designs:
123	
124	> Two kits contributing a [registration entry] for the same key —
125	> including a plugin's own self-registered defaults — refuse to start
126	> the guild; the startup error names both contributing plugins and
127	> the conflicting key.
128	
129	Silent first-or-last-wins is rejected because a typo in a source string
130	would silently corrupt the registry — exactly the failure mode the
131	fail-loud rule exists to surface.
132	
133	---
134	
135	## 2. Petitioner identity
136	
137	The Reckoner maintains a closed registry of petitioner sources. Only a
138	source registered through `registerPetitioner` may emit; an unknown
139	source at emit time is unreachable through the handle surface (see §3)
140	and any direct intake call carrying an unregistered source is
141	rejected fail-loud — there is no silent fallback. The closure stance
142	is free: the registry already exists for authority-gating metadata
143	(see §7), so closing it merely refuses unannounced sources rather than
144	silently letting them propagate. This is the same trade-off the
145	[Clerk's writ-type registry](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry)
146	takes — closure plus a fail-loud "Registered sources: …" diagnostic
147	beats silent acceptance every time.
148	
149	### Source-id grammar
150	
151	A source id has the form **`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`** — the
152	contributing plugin's derived id, a literal `.`, then a kebab-case
153	suffix (lowercase letters, digits, and single-hyphen separators; no
154	leading or trailing hyphen). The grammar matches
155	[Lattice trigger-types](apparatus/lattice.md) and
156	[Clerk link-kinds](apparatus/clerk.md#kit-interface). Examples:
157	
158	- `vision-keeper.snapshot`
159	- `vision-keeper.drift-detected`
160	- `coinmaster.balance-low` (illustrative future consumer)
161	
162	The `{pluginId}.` prefix is validated at registration time wherever the
163	plugin id is derivable from the call site — same pattern the
164	[Clerk uses for link-kind ids](apparatus/clerk.md#linkkinds-registry):
165	malformed ids and mismatched prefixes hard-fail at the registration
166	call, never at first emit. Cross-plugin collisions are made structurally
167	impossible by the prefix rule and operationally impossible by the
168	duplicate-registration check in §1.
169	
170	The one hard-coded exception is **`'patron'`** — a bare, prefix-free
171	identifier reserved for the patron's own elevated emit path. The
172	Reckoner self-registers this source from its own `start()` (see §6),
173	so the exception is internal to the apparatus that enforces the
174	gate — third-party plugins can never reach this name through the
175	public API.
176	
177	### Source authentication — handle-based
178	
179	The source field on every emitted petition is **never passed as a
180	string by callers**. `registerPetitioner` returns a typed
181	`PetitionerHandle` (see §3) whose `emit` and `withdraw` methods stamp
182	the handle's bound source field internally. The caller's only path
183	to emit is through the handle returned by their own registration
184	call, so the `source` field on a petition is mechanically guaranteed
185	to match the registering plugin.
186	
187	This is a **deliberate divergence** from the
188	[Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md)'s emitter-trust model, where
189	`pulse.source` is a string the emitter stamps and the substrate
190	trusts (per the Lattice's D21). Petitions carry authority weight
191	(`priority=immediate` is gated on `source=patron`; see §7), so
192	trusting an emitter-supplied string would let a typo in *any*
193	emit call site silently elevate authority. With handle-based
194	stamping a misnamed source becomes a TypeScript error or an
195	unreachable call path, not a runtime authority drift. The handle is
196	also the natural carrier for petitioner-scoped withdraw (see §3).
197	The handle-vs-trust-stamp tradeoff is named in
198	[Open Questions](#open-questions) for the case where a non-handle-
199	reachable caller eventually appears.
200	
201	---
202	
203	## 3. Emit-petition interface
204	
205	### `PetitionerHandle`
206	
207	`registerPetitioner` returns a typed handle. The handle is the **sole**
208	runtime surface a petitioner uses to interact with the Reckoner —
209	there is no global `reckoner.emitAs(source, …)` shortcut, no string-
210	keyed lookup, and no way to recover a handle from outside the
211	registering plugin's `start()`.
212	
213	```typescript
214	interface PetitionerHandle {
215	  /** The source string this handle stamps onto every emit/withdraw. */
216	  readonly source: string;
217	
218	  /** The priority levels this handle is permitted to emit at. */
219	  readonly allowedPriorities: readonly PetitionPriority[];
220	
221	  /**
222	   * Emit a petition. Returns the persisted PetitionDoc.
223	   * The caller does NOT pass `source`; the handle stamps it.
224	   */
225	  emit(request: EmitPetitionRequest): Promise<PetitionDoc>;
226	
227	  /**
228	   * Withdraw a previously-emitted petition. The handle's source
229	   * must match the petition's source — cross-source withdraw throws.
230	   */
231	  withdraw(petitionId: string, reason?: string): Promise<PetitionDoc>;
232	}
233	```
234	
235	The handle's `readonly source` and `readonly allowedPriorities` fields
236	are diagnostic — useful inside the registering plugin for logging and
237	for sanity-checking the inputs to a `with:` block — but they are not
238	the binding mechanism. The binding is closure: `emit` and `withdraw`
239	close over the registration record at the moment `registerPetitioner`
240	returns, and the registration record is the one the registry validates
241	against on every call.
242	
243	### `emit` — call shape and return type
244	
245	The emit call carries the petition payload minus the source field
246	(stamped by the handle) and minus any lifecycle metadata (set by the
247	Reckoner on persist):
248	
249	```typescript
250	interface EmitPetitionRequest {
251	  /** What the petitioner wants the Reckoner to schedule. */
252	  intent: string;
253	
254	  /** Priority — must appear in this handle's allowedPriorities. */
255	  priority: PetitionPriority;
256	
257	  /** Optional structured payload — petitioner-defined shape. */
258	  context?: Record<string, unknown>;
259	
260	  /** Optional writ binding — when non-null, the petition is writ-scoped. */
261	  writId?: string | null;
262	}
263	
264	handle.emit({ intent, priority, context, writId }):
265	  Promise<PetitionDoc>
266	```
267	
268	Note the absence of any `source` parameter — there is no place in this
269	contract where a caller passes a `source` string to emit. Every
270	example in this document is handle-rooted for the same reason.
271	
272	`emit` is **final-on-call**. There is no draft phase, no
273	`emitDraft()`, no `handle.partial()`, and no commit step. The
274	petition lands in the Reckoner's `pending` state immediately and
275	becomes visible to the scheduler on its next tick. v0 deliberately
276	omits the draft surface: the petition shape is already coarse, and
277	adding a draft state would duplicate the [Clerk's `new` phase](apparatus/clerk.md#mandates-lifecycle-an-example-registered-type)
278	without a named consumer to motivate the second-write step.
279	Petitioners that need to assemble data across multiple steps assemble
280	it before calling `emit`. Adding drafts later is purely additive — a
281	future `handle.draft(...)` returning a draft handle would not
282	invalidate any v0 caller — and is named in
283	[Open Questions](#open-questions) as a deferred consideration.
284	
285	The emit return type is the **full persisted `PetitionDoc`** — same
286	shape the Reckoner stores in the petitions book, including its
287	generated id, timestamps, lifecycle state, and the Reckoner-stamped
288	fields the petitioner did not supply. This mirrors
289	[`ClerkApi.post`](apparatus/clerk.md#clerkapi-interface-provides) and
290	[`LatticeApi.emit`](apparatus/lattice.md#latticeapi-interface-provides):
291	both return the complete persisted document so the caller can chain
292	reads, log the resulting id, or pin observability without a second
293	lookup. A handle-tuple shape (`{ id, ack }`) was considered and
294	rejected — neither adjacent API earns a richer return shape, and
295	handle returns are unearned generality.
296	
297	### `withdraw` — petitioner-initiated
298	
299	A petitioner may withdraw a petition it previously issued by calling
300	`withdraw` on the **same handle** that issued it:
301	
302	```typescript
303	await handle.withdraw(petitionId, 'Snapshot superseded by drift '
304	                                + 'detected before this one ran.');
305	```
306	
307	The withdraw call's contract:
308	
309	- **Handle-scoped.** The Reckoner asserts that the petition's stored
310	  `source` matches the handle's bound source. A petitioner trying to
311	  withdraw another petitioner's petition throws fail-loud — the lookup
312	  is structurally one-source-only. "Only the issuing source can
313	  withdraw" is enforced by reachability, not by a runtime authority
314	  check.
315	- **Allowed only from non-terminal states.** A petition can be
316	  withdrawn while it is in `new`, `pending`, or `deferred`. Petitions
317	  in any terminal state — already accepted, declined, executed, or
318	  withdrawn — reject the call. The terminal-state list is the
319	  petition lifecycle's own (per `c-modaqnpt`); withdraw lands the
320	  petition in the lifecycle's petitioner-initiated `withdrawn`
321	  terminal.
322	- **Optional reason.** A short freeform string surfaced on the
323	  resulting decline-feedback channel and persisted on the petition
324	  document. Omitting it is allowed; passing one helps the standing-
325	  order recipes in §4 explain themselves to the patron.
326	- **Returns the updated `PetitionDoc`.** Same shape as `emit`'s
327	  return — the post-transition snapshot, useful for logging the
328	  resolved-at timestamp without a second read.
329	
330	There is no Reckoner-initiated withdraw call exposed on the handle.
331	The Reckoner's own decline / defer transitions are observed through
332	the feedback recipes in §4; they are not invoked by the petitioner.
333	
334	---
335	
336	## 4. Feedback receipt patterns
337	
338	A petitioner observes what happened to its petitions through
339	**[Clockworks](clockworks.md) standing orders** keyed on the
340	auto-wired CDC events the Reckonings book emits. There is no
341	`handle.onStateChange(callback)` API and no in-process subscribe
342	surface on the Reckoner — the v0 conclusion (per `c-mod9a54n`) is
343	that a parallel handle-subscribe path would duplicate the substrate
344	that already exists, lack durability and replay across daemon
345	restarts, and add an invocation-ordering contract the framework has
346	not yet earned. Standing orders are durable (the trigger lives in
347	`guild.json`), survive process restarts, and slot into the same
348	event-routing fabric every other apparatus uses.
349	
350	The Reckonings book is owned by the Reckoner and its CDC events are
351	auto-wired by [Clockworks](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
352	through the standard
353	`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` pattern — the
354	same substrate every other plugin's books ride on. (The book exists
355	and is CDC-attached; its specific record fields and payload schema
356	are owned by the parallel Reckonings-book commission, per
357	`c-modeou1t`. This contract relies only on the existence of CDC
358	events keyed by the petition's `source`, not on any specific
359	schema field.)
360	
361	A petitioner picks one of three opt-in feedback channels.
362	
363	### Channel 1 — event-driven standing order (the canonical path)
364	
365	The recommended channel. The petitioner declares a standing order in
366	`guild.json` keyed on the petitions book's `updated` event, filtered
367	to its own source via the standard standing-order filter the order
368	hands to its relay through `with:`. The relay reads the post-commit
369	event payload, decides whether the lifecycle change is one the
370	petitioner cares about, and re-emits / cleans up / notifies as
371	appropriate.
372	
373	The vision-keeper's decline-watch recipe:
374	
375	```jsonc
376	{
377	  "clockworks": {
378	    "standingOrders": [
379	      {
380	        "on": "book.reckoner.petitions.updated",
381	        "run": "vision-keeper-on-decline",
382	        "with": {
383	          "filterSource": "vision-keeper.snapshot",
384	          "filterTerminalAttr": "declined"
385	        }
386	      }
387	    ]
388	  }
389	}
390	```
391	
392	The relay's handler (a vision-keeper-side relay contributed via the
393	plugin's [`ClockworksKit`](clockworks.md#clockworkskit)) inspects the
394	event payload at handler time:
395	
396	```typescript
397	// vision-keeper/src/relays/on-decline.ts
398	import { relay } from '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus';
399	
400	export default relay({
401	  name: 'vision-keeper-on-decline',
402	  handler: async (event, { params }) => {
403	    const entry  = event?.payload?.entry as { source?: string; ... };
404	    if (!entry || entry.source !== params.filterSource) return;
405	    // Inspect the entry's lifecycle attrs / decline reason and re-emit
406	    // the snapshot petition with adjusted context, log to the keeper's
407	    // own book, ping a Lattice channel — whatever the keeper does on
408	    // a decline. The handle (closed over from the keeper's start())
409	    // is reachable through `guild().apparatus(...)` for the keeper's
410	    // own runtime API.
411	  },
412	});
413	```
414	
415	Two things worth highlighting. First, the filter on
416	`event.payload.entry.source` is the load-bearing one — every
417	petition-book CDC event carries the entry as its payload, and the
418	source field is what scopes the keeper's relay to its own petitions
419	without coupling to other plugins' sources. Second, the standing
420	order points at a relay the keeper itself ships in its
421	`supportKit.relays`; standing orders never name the petitioner
422	plugin directly.
423	
424	The `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` event name is the auto-wired
425	form (`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.updated`) — the petitioner does not
426	need to declare the event in `clockworks.events`; it is supplied by
427	the Stacks-Clockworks bridge automatically.
428	
429	### Channel 2 — polling the petitions book
430	
431	For petitioners that need point-in-time aggregate state (a status
432	dashboard, a periodic reconciliation pass, a quick "do I have
433	anything outstanding?" check), the channel is a direct read of the
434	Reckonings book filtered by the petitioner's own source:
435	
436	```typescript
437	// In a vision-keeper-owned tool or scheduled relay handler:
438	const stacks  = guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
439	const book    = stacks.book<PetitionDoc>('reckoner', 'petitions');
440	const mine    = await book.list({
441	  where: [['source', '=', 'vision-keeper.snapshot']],
442	});
443	const pending = mine.filter((p) => p.lifecycleClassification !== 'terminal');
444	```
445	
446	Polling is appropriate when the petitioner wants a snapshot rather
447	than a stream — daily reports, reconciliation, ad-hoc inspection.
448	For event-by-event reaction, prefer Channel 1 — polling cannot
449	distinguish a transition from steady state without keeping its own
450	side-channel state.
451	
452	### Channel 3 — fire-and-forget
453	
454	The third channel is no channel at all: the petitioner emits and
455	walks away. Acceptance, decline, defer, or successful execution all
456	happen without the petitioner ever observing the outcome. Re-emission
457	is driven by the petitioner's own conditions — when the same
458	condition triggers again, the petitioner emits again; the Reckoner's
459	lifecycle (per `c-modaqnpt`) handles dedupe / supersede on its own.
460	
461	Fire-and-forget is the right channel for petitioners whose intent is
462	"I noticed *X* happened; route this to the scheduler if it matters."
463	A `vision-keeper.snapshot` triggered hourly by a cron-like relay can
464	emit without subscribing — if it is declined as a duplicate of an
465	in-flight snapshot petition, the next hour's tick will emit again
466	when the keeper's conditions re-evaluate. The petitioner never has
467	to encode lifecycle reactions; the cadence handles it.
468	
469	### Channel selection — recommended order
470	
471	| Need | Channel |
472	|------|---------|
473	| React to declines / defers / completions in real time | **1 — standing order** |
474	| Periodic reconciliation, dashboards, "what's outstanding?" | **2 — polling** |
475	| "I noticed *X*; route it" with no follow-up | **3 — fire-and-forget** |
476	
477	A petitioner is free to combine channels (a dashboard tool polling +
478	a relay reacting to declines) — they observe the same underlying
479	book through the same auto-wired CDC substrate. The recipes do not
480	compete; they slice the same data different ways.
481	
482	---
483	
484	## 5. Lifecycle hooks
485	
486	**v0 declares no lifecycle hooks.** A petitioner's full declarative
487	surface is the three-field `RegisterPetitionerRequest` shape from §1
488	(`source`, `description`, and the optional `allowedPriorities` whose
489	default is `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`):
490	
491	```typescript
492	interface RegisterPetitionerRequest {
493	  source:             string;
494	  allowedPriorities?: PetitionPriority[];
495	  description:        string;
496	}
497	```
498	
499	There is no `onAccept`, no `canRetry`, no `onDefer`, no `onWithdraw`,

file.content:
The petitioner registry seals at the framework's global
`phase:started` signal — the moment every apparatus's `start()` has
finished. After the seal, every `registerPetitioner` call throws
with a clear `[reckoner] registerPetitioner: cannot register
petitioner "<source>" — the startup registration window has closed.`
diagnostic, naming the offending source. This matches the writ-type
seal in the [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry): the
"apparatus startup is the registration window" invariant is framework-
wide, not Reckoner-specific. By the time the first `crawl()` /
scheduler tick runs, the registry is inspectable and immutable.

### Kit-vs-kit collision policy

Two `registerPetitioner` calls with the same `source` string are a
**hard error**. The second call throws at registration time with a
diagnostic that names *both* contributing plugin ids and the
conflicting source — the winner is never selected by load order. This
mirrors the framework-wide rule the Spider documents for
`rigTemplateMappings` (per `c-mod9a6x3`'s lineage) and that
[Clerk applies to writ-types](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry),
[Spider to `blockTypes` / `rigTemplateMappings`](apparatus/spider.md#plugin-default-template-and-mapping),
and the Fabricator to engine designs:

> Two kits contributing a [registration entry] for the same key —
> including a plugin's own self-registered defaults — refuse to start
> the guild; the startup error names both contributing plugins and
> the conflicting key.

Silent first-or-last-wins is rejected because a typo in a source string
would silently corrupt the registry — exactly the failure mode the
fail-loud rule exists to surface.

---

## 2. Petitioner identity

The Reckoner maintains a closed registry of petitioner sources. Only a
source registered through `registerPetitioner` may emit; an unknown
source at emit time is unreachable through the handle surface (see §3)
and any direct intake call carrying an unregistered source is
rejected fail-loud — there is no silent fallback. The closure stance
is free: the registry already exists for authority-gating metadata
(see §7), so closing it merely refuses unannounced sources rather than
silently letting them propagate. This is the same trade-off the
[Clerk's writ-type registry](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry)
takes — closure plus a fail-loud "Registered sources: …" diagnostic
beats silent acceptance every time.

### Source-id grammar

A source id has the form **`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`** — the
contributing plugin's derived id, a literal `.`, then a kebab-case
suffix (lowercase letters, digits, and single-hyphen separators; no
leading or trailing hyphen). The grammar matches
[Lattice trigger-types](apparatus/lattice.md) and
[Clerk link-kinds](apparatus/clerk.md#kit-interface). Examples:

- `vision-keeper.snapshot`
- `vision-keeper.drift-detected`
- `coinmaster.balance-low` (illustrative future consumer)

The `{pluginId}.` prefix is validated at registration time wherever the
plugin id is derivable from the call site — same pattern the
[Clerk uses for link-kind ids](apparatus/clerk.md#linkkinds-registry):
malformed ids and mismatched prefixes hard-fail at the registration
call, never at first emit. Cross-plugin collisions are made structurally
impossible by the prefix rule and operationally impossible by the
duplicate-registration check in §1.

The one hard-coded exception is **`'patron'`** — a bare, prefix-free
identifier reserved for the patron's own elevated emit path. The
Reckoner self-registers this source from its own `start()` (see §6),
so the exception is internal to the apparatus that enforces the
gate — third-party plugins can never reach this name through the
public API.

### Source authentication — handle-based

The source field on every emitted petition is **never passed as a
string by callers**. `registerPetitioner` returns a typed
`PetitionerHandle` (see §3) whose `emit` and `withdraw` methods stamp
the handle's bound source field internally. The caller's only path
to emit is through the handle returned by their own registration
call, so the `source` field on a petition is mechanically guaranteed
to match the registering plugin.

This is a **deliberate divergence** from the
[Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md)'s emitter-trust model, where
`pulse.source` is a string the emitter stamps and the substrate
trusts (per the Lattice's D21). Petitions carry authority weight
(`priority=immediate` is gated on `source=patron`; see §7), so
trusting an emitter-supplied string would let a typo in *any*
emit call site silently elevate authority. With handle-based
stamping a misnamed source becomes a TypeScript error or an
unreachable call path, not a runtime authority drift. The handle is
also the natural carrier for petitioner-scoped withdraw (see §3).
The handle-vs-trust-stamp tradeoff is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions) for the case where a non-handle-
reachable caller eventually appears.

---

## 3. Emit-petition interface

### `PetitionerHandle`

`registerPetitioner` returns a typed handle. The handle is the **sole**
runtime surface a petitioner uses to interact with the Reckoner —
there is no global `reckoner.emitAs(source, …)` shortcut, no string-
keyed lookup, and no way to recover a handle from outside the
registering plugin's `start()`.

```typescript
interface PetitionerHandle {
  /** The source string this handle stamps onto every emit/withdraw. */
  readonly source: string;

  /** The priority levels this handle is permitted to emit at. */
  readonly allowedPriorities: readonly PetitionPriority[];

  /**
   * Emit a petition. Returns the persisted PetitionDoc.
   * The caller does NOT pass `source`; the handle stamps it.
   */
  emit(request: EmitPetitionRequest): Promise<PetitionDoc>;

  /**
   * Withdraw a previously-emitted petition. The handle's source
   * must match the petition's source — cross-source withdraw throws.
   */
  withdraw(petitionId: string, reason?: string): Promise<PetitionDoc>;
}
```

The handle's `readonly source` and `readonly allowedPriorities` fields
are diagnostic — useful inside the registering plugin for logging and
for sanity-checking the inputs to a `with:` block — but they are not
the binding mechanism. The binding is closure: `emit` and `withdraw`
close over the registration record at the moment `registerPetitioner`
returns, and the registration record is the one the registry validates
against on every call.

### `emit` — call shape and return type

The emit call carries the petition payload minus the source field
(stamped by the handle) and minus any lifecycle metadata (set by the
Reckoner on persist):

```typescript
interface EmitPetitionRequest {
  /** What the petitioner wants the Reckoner to schedule. */
  intent: string;

  /** Priority — must appear in this handle's allowedPriorities. */
  priority: PetitionPriority;

  /** Optional structured payload — petitioner-defined shape. */
  context?: Record<string, unknown>;

  /** Optional writ binding — when non-null, the petition is writ-scoped. */
  writId?: string | null;
}

handle.emit({ intent, priority, context, writId }):
  Promise<PetitionDoc>
```

Note the absence of any `source` parameter — there is no place in this
contract where a caller passes a `source` string to emit. Every
example in this document is handle-rooted for the same reason.

`emit` is **final-on-call**. There is no draft phase, no
`emitDraft()`, no `handle.partial()`, and no commit step. The
petition lands in the Reckoner's `pending` state immediately and
becomes visible to the scheduler on its next tick. v0 deliberately
omits the draft surface: the petition shape is already coarse, and
adding a draft state would duplicate the [Clerk's `new` phase](apparatus/clerk.md#mandates-lifecycle-an-example-registered-type)
without a named consumer to motivate the second-write step.
Petitioners that need to assemble data across multiple steps assemble
it before calling `emit`. Adding drafts later is purely additive — a
future `handle.draft(...)` returning a draft handle would not
invalidate any v0 caller — and is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions) as a deferred consideration.

The emit return type is the **full persisted `PetitionDoc`** — same
shape the Reckoner stores in the petitions book, including its
generated id, timestamps, lifecycle state, and the Reckoner-stamped
fields the petitioner did not supply. This mirrors
[`ClerkApi.post`](apparatus/clerk.md#clerkapi-interface-provides) and
[`LatticeApi.emit`](apparatus/lattice.md#latticeapi-interface-provides):
both return the complete persisted document so the caller can chain
reads, log the resulting id, or pin observability without a second
lookup. A handle-tuple shape (`{ id, ack }`) was considered and
rejected — neither adjacent API earns a richer return shape, and
handle returns are unearned generality.

### `withdraw` — petitioner-initiated

A petitioner may withdraw a petition it previously issued by calling
`withdraw` on the **same handle** that issued it:

```typescript
await handle.withdraw(petitionId, 'Snapshot superseded by drift '
                                + 'detected before this one ran.');
```

The withdraw call's contract:

- **Handle-scoped.** The Reckoner asserts that the petition's stored
  `source` matches the handle's bound source. A petitioner trying to
  withdraw another petitioner's petition throws fail-loud — the lookup
  is structurally one-source-only. "Only the issuing source can
  withdraw" is enforced by reachability, not by a runtime authority
  check.
- **Allowed only from non-terminal states.** A petition can be
  withdrawn while it is in `new`, `pending`, or `deferred`. Petitions
  in any terminal state — already accepted, declined, executed, or
  withdrawn — reject the call. The terminal-state list is the
  petition lifecycle's own (per `c-modaqnpt`); withdraw lands the
  petition in the lifecycle's petitioner-initiated `withdrawn`
  terminal.
- **Optional reason.** A short freeform string surfaced on the
  resulting decline-feedback channel and persisted on the petition
  document. Omitting it is allowed; passing one helps the standing-
  order recipes in §4 explain themselves to the patron.
- **Returns the updated `PetitionDoc`.** Same shape as `emit`'s
  return — the post-transition snapshot, useful for logging the
  resolved-at timestamp without a second read.

There is no Reckoner-initiated withdraw call exposed on the handle.
The Reckoner's own decline / defer transitions are observed through
the feedback recipes in §4; they are not invoked by the petitioner.

---

## 4. Feedback receipt patterns

A petitioner observes what happened to its petitions through
**[Clockworks](clockworks.md) standing orders** keyed on the
auto-wired CDC events the Reckonings book emits. There is no
`handle.onStateChange(callback)` API and no in-process subscribe
surface on the Reckoner — the v0 conclusion (per `c-mod9a54n`) is
that a parallel handle-subscribe path would duplicate the substrate
that already exists, lack durability and replay across daemon
restarts, and add an invocation-ordering contract the framework has
not yet earned. Standing orders are durable (the trigger lives in
`guild.json`), survive process restarts, and slot into the same
event-routing fabric every other apparatus uses.

The Reckonings book is owned by the Reckoner and its CDC events are
auto-wired by [Clockworks](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
through the standard
`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` pattern — the
same substrate every other plugin's books ride on. (The book exists
and is CDC-attached; its specific record fields and payload schema
are owned by the parallel Reckonings-book commission, per
`c-modeou1t`. This contract relies only on the existence of CDC
events keyed by the petition's `source`, not on any specific
schema field.)

A petitioner picks one of three opt-in feedback channels.

### Channel 1 — event-driven standing order (the canonical path)

The recommended channel. The petitioner declares a standing order in
`guild.json` keyed on the petitions book's `updated` event, filtered
to its own source via the standard standing-order filter the order
hands to its relay through `with:`. The relay reads the post-commit
event payload, decides whether the lifecycle change is one the
petitioner cares about, and re-emits / cleans up / notifies as
appropriate.

The vision-keeper's decline-watch recipe:

```jsonc
{
  "clockworks": {
    "standingOrders": [
      {
        "on": "book.reckoner.petitions.updated",
        "run": "vision-keeper-on-decline",
        "with": {
          "filterSource": "vision-keeper.snapshot",
          "filterTerminalAttr": "declined"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The relay's handler (a vision-keeper-side relay contributed via the
plugin's [`ClockworksKit`](clockworks.md#clockworkskit)) inspects the
event payload at handler time:

```typescript
// vision-keeper/src/relays/on-decline.ts
import { relay } from '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus';

export default relay({
  name: 'vision-keeper-on-decline',
  handler: async (event, { params }) => {
    const entry  = event?.payload?.entry as { source?: string; ... };
    if (!entry || entry.source !== params.filterSource) return;
    // Inspect the entry's lifecycle attrs / decline reason and re-emit
    // the snapshot petition with adjusted context, log to the keeper's
    // own book, ping a Lattice channel — whatever the keeper does on
    // a decline. The handle (closed over from the keeper's start())
    // is reachable through `guild().apparatus(...)` for the keeper's
    // own runtime API.
  },
});
```

Two things worth highlighting. First, the filter on
`event.payload.entry.source` is the load-bearing one — every
petition-book CDC event carries the entry as its payload, and the
source field is what scopes the keeper's relay to its own petitions
without coupling to other plugins' sources. Second, the standing
order points at a relay the keeper itself ships in its
`supportKit.relays`; standing orders never name the petitioner
plugin directly.

The `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` event name is the auto-wired
form (`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.updated`) — the petitioner does not
need to declare the event in `clockworks.events`; it is supplied by
the Stacks-Clockworks bridge automatically.

### Channel 2 — polling the petitions book

For petitioners that need point-in-time aggregate state (a status
dashboard, a periodic reconciliation pass, a quick "do I have
anything outstanding?" check), the channel is a direct read of the
Reckonings book filtered by the petitioner's own source:

```typescript
// In a vision-keeper-owned tool or scheduled relay handler:
const stacks  = guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
const book    = stacks.book<PetitionDoc>('reckoner', 'petitions');
const mine    = await book.list({
  where: [['source', '=', 'vision-keeper.snapshot']],
});
const pending = mine.filter((p) => p.lifecycleClassification !== 'terminal');
```

Polling is appropriate when the petitioner wants a snapshot rather
than a stream — daily reports, reconciliation, ad-hoc inspection.
For event-by-event reaction, prefer Channel 1 — polling cannot
distinguish a transition from steady state without keeping its own
side-channel state.

### Channel 3 — fire-and-forget

The third channel is no channel at all: the petitioner emits and
walks away. Acceptance, decline, defer, or successful execution all
happen without the petitioner ever observing the outcome. Re-emission
is driven by the petitioner's own conditions — when the same
condition triggers again, the petitioner emits again; the Reckoner's
lifecycle (per `c-modaqnpt`) handles dedupe / supersede on its own.

Fire-and-forget is the right channel for petitioners whose intent is
"I noticed *X* happened; route this to the scheduler if it matters."
A `vision-keeper.snapshot` triggered hourly by a cron-like relay can
emit without subscribing — if it is declined as a duplicate of an
in-flight snapshot petition, the next hour's tick will emit again
when the keeper's conditions re-evaluate. The petitioner never has
to encode lifecycle reactions; the cadence handles it.

### Channel selection — recommended order

| Need | Channel |
|------|---------|
| React to declines / defers / completions in real time | **1 — standing order** |
| Periodic reconciliation, dashboards, "what's outstanding?" | **2 — polling** |
| "I noticed *X*; route it" with no follow-up | **3 — fire-and-forget** |

A petitioner is free to combine channels (a dashboard tool polling +
a relay reacting to declines) — they observe the same underlying
book through the same auto-wired CDC substrate. The recipes do not
compete; they slice the same data different ways.

---

## 5. Lifecycle hooks

**v0 declares no lifecycle hooks.** A petitioner's full declarative
surface is the three-field `RegisterPetitionerRequest` shape from §1
(`source`, `description`, and the optional `allowedPriorities` whose
default is `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`):

```typescript
interface RegisterPetitionerRequest {
  source:             string;
  allowedPriorities?: PetitionPriority[];
  description:        string;
}
```

There is no `onAccept`, no `canRetry`, no `onDefer`, no `onWithdraw`,

content.content:
500	no per-petitioner intake validator, and no callback that runs on the
501	Reckoner's process before a petition is persisted. The upstream
502	conclusion (per `c-mod9a48y`) was that a hook surface is "no direct
503	callback mechanism in v0," and this contract makes that final.
504	
505	The reasoning is twofold: the observation need is already covered by
506	the CDC + standing-order recipes in §4 without an additional
507	invocation-ordering contract, and pre-empting hooks now keeps the
508	petition lifecycle's observe-and-re-emit pattern uniform across every
509	petitioner. A hook surface that runs in the Reckoner's process would
510	introduce ordering questions (does `canRetry` race with the parent's
511	update event? does an `onDefer` throw block the defer transition?)
512	that the framework has no precedent to lean on.
513	
514	**Deferred hook surface.** The most-likely future hooks are named
515	in [Open Questions](#open-questions) — `canRetry`, `onDefer`,
516	`onAccept` are the obvious candidates — and re-evaluation is gated on
517	a real consumer with a concrete need that CDC + re-emit cannot
518	satisfy. Adding a hook later is additive: a future
519	`registerPetitioner({ source, allowedPriorities, description, onDefer })`
520	shape does not invalidate any v0 caller.
521	
522	---
523	
524	## 6. Built-in petitioner classes
525	
526	v0 ships with exactly **two** built-in petitioner classes. Both are
527	the smallest set the contract needs to be coherent and testable.
528	
529	### `'patron'`
530	
531	The patron's elevated petition source. Registered by the **Reckoner
532	itself** from its own `start()` — the Reckoner is the apparatus that
533	enforces the authority gate (see §7), so self-registering the one
534	identity that gate requires keeps the invariant local. This mirrors
535	the [Clerk's self-registration of `mandate`](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry):
536	the framework's one mandatory registry entry is contributed by the
537	apparatus that owns the registry, not by an external kit.
538	
539	The patron source is the hard-coded exception to the
540	`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` grammar from §2. The bare `'patron'`
541	identifier is reserved; third-party plugins cannot register it
542	(blocked by the duplicate-source check in §1) and cannot reach the
543	Reckoner-issued patron handle.
544	
545	How the patron *invokes* emit — the CLI surface, the MCP tool, the
546	`commission-post` interaction — is **not** part of this contract. A
547	downstream commission designs the patron-emit surface against the
548	internally-issued handle. This document fixes only how `'patron'` is
549	*registered*, not how the patron *invokes* emit.
550	
551	### `'vision-keeper'`
552	
553	The canonical worked example of a third-party petitioner. The
554	vision-keeper exercises every contract surface in this doc:
555	
556	- It registers via `registerPetitioner` from its own apparatus's
557	  `start()` (§1).
558	- It declares a qualified source id `vision-keeper.snapshot` (§2).
559	- It calls `handle.emit(...)` with `priority: 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low'`
560	  drawn from the default allow-list (§7).
561	- It withdraws superseded petitions through `handle.withdraw(...)` (§3).
562	- It observes outcomes through a Channel-1 standing order on the
563	  petitions book filtered by its own source (§4).
564	
565	Every example in this document is calibrated against the keeper.
566	
567	### Future consumers — out of scope for v0
568	
569	The renamed pulse-emitter (the apparatus currently documented as the
570	[Reckoner](apparatus/reckoner.md)'s queue-observer) is named **only
571	as a future consumer**, not a v0 deliverable. Its rename and
572	subsume-later track is a separate commission; pulling it into this
573	contract would conflate the two. It is named in
574	[Open Questions](#open-questions) for completeness.
575	
576	### Shared base classes / utility packages — recipes only
577	
578	v0 documents shared registration patterns as **worked recipes**, not
579	as exported base classes or shared utility packages. There is no
580	`PetitionerBase` to extend, no `mountPetitioner()` helper, no
581	`@shardworks/petitioner-utils` package. Every petitioner writes the
582	five-line registration call from §1 against the Reckoner API directly.
583	
584	Two consumers (`patron` + `vision-keeper`) is below the threshold
585	where the right shape for a base class becomes obvious — base-class
586	extraction is the kind of refactor a third petitioner usually
587	motivates. The earned-extraction stance is named in
588	[Open Questions](#open-questions).
589	
590	---
591	
592	## 7. Authority / priority gating
593	
594	The `priority=immediate` level is reserved for elevated authority,
595	and the gate that enforces this is **registration metadata**, not a
596	hard-coded `if (source === 'patron')` check inside the intake
597	validator.
598	
599	Every `registerPetitioner` call carries an `allowedPriorities`
600	allow-list (optional in the descriptor; `undefined` resolves to the
601	default `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`):
602	
603	```typescript
604	type PetitionPriority = 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';
605	
606	interface RegisterPetitionerRequest {
607	  source:             string;
608	  allowedPriorities?: PetitionPriority[];
609	  description:        string;
610	}
611	```
612	
613	The Reckoner's own `start()` registers `'patron'` with the full
614	allow-list:
615	
616	```typescript
617	// inside reckoner apparatus start()
618	registerPetitionerInternal({
619	  source: 'patron',
620	  allowedPriorities: ['immediate', 'urgent', 'normal', 'low'],
621	  description: 'Elevated patron-originated petitions.',
622	}, 'builtin');
623	```
624	
625	Every other registration (third-party plugins calling the public
626	`registerPetitioner`) **defaults to** `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`
627	when the allow-list is omitted, and the Reckoner refuses any
628	registration that includes `'immediate'` for any source other than
629	the self-registered `'patron'`. The refusal is enforced at
630	registration time, not at first emit — a third-party plugin trying
631	to register `allowedPriorities: ['immediate', ...]` fails the
632	guild's startup with a `[reckoner] registerPetitioner: priority
633	"immediate" is reserved for the patron source` diagnostic.
634	
635	The emit-time validator (the one that runs inside `handle.emit(...)`)
636	reads the allow-list from the **registry** for the handle's bound
637	source — there is no source-name comparison in the validator's body,
638	no `if (req.source === 'patron')` branch, and no special case for
639	the patron string. A petition arrives with a priority; the validator
640	asks the registry "is this priority in this source's allow-list?";
641	yes admits the petition, no throws fail-loud.
642	
643	Two properties fall out of this design:
644	
645	- **The constraint is queryable.** Operators (and the keeper-side
646	  diagnostic logging) can read `handle.allowedPriorities` to see
647	  what the petitioner is permitted to emit at, without parsing the
648	  validator's source code.
649	- **The gate generalizes to future elevated sources.** If a future
650	  apparatus needs to emit at `'immediate'` (e.g. a "guild-emergency-
651	  drain" source), the design space is "the Reckoner registers a
652	  second internal source with the full allow-list" — the validator
653	  does not change. There is no domain-specific name baked into the
654	  gate.
655	
656	---
657	
658	## 8. Existing precedents
659	
660	The petitioner registration contract is not new framework shape — it
661	composes patterns the framework's adjacent apparatuses have already
662	settled. This section names *what fits* and *what's different* for
663	each precedent the contract draws from.
664	
665	### [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md) writ-types
666	
667	**What fits.** Clerk's writ-type registry is the closest live
668	precedent — same call shape, same seal timing, same fail-loud-on-
669	duplicate stance, same self-registration of the framework's one
670	mandatory entry. Specifically:
671	
672	- `ClerkApi.registerWritType` ↔ `ReckonerApi.registerPetitioner` —
673	  programmatic, single-surface, called from the contributing
674	  apparatus's `start()`.
675	- `phase:started` registry seal ↔ identical seal timing here.
676	- "Two plugins cannot contribute the same writ type name" ↔
677	  identical duplicate-source policy here.
678	- The Clerk's self-registration of `mandate` ↔ the Reckoner's self-
679	  registration of `'patron'` (per §6).
680	
681	**What's different.** Writ types are fully static descriptors
682	(`WritTypeConfig` is a pure data shape — states, classifications,
683	attrs, transitions). Petitioner registrations bind a runtime closure:
684	the returned `PetitionerHandle` carries `emit` and `withdraw` closures
685	that the registering plugin calls into. The writ-type registry has
686	no per-type runtime handle returned to the caller; the Clerk's
687	public surface is the same `ClerkApi.transition(id, …)` for every
688	type.
689	
690	### [Spider](apparatus/spider.md) rig-template mappings
691	
692	**What fits.** The framework-wide kit-vs-kit collision policy is the
693	shape this contract adopts in §1: a duplicate `registerPetitioner`
694	with the same source string is a hard startup error that names both
695	contributing plugins, identical to the rule the Spider documents for
696	[`rigTemplateMappings`](apparatus/spider.md#plugin-default-template-and-mapping)
697	and that applies framework-wide to Clerk `writTypes`, Spider
698	`blockTypes`, and Fabricator engines. The winner is never selected
699	by load order.
700	
701	**What's different.** Spider's rig-template mappings have a
702	config-overlay tier: a `guild.json` `spider.rigTemplateMappings`
703	entry overrides any kit contribution silently. The petitioner
704	registry has **no config tier** — there is no `reckoner.petitioners`
705	section in `guild.json` — because the registration is runtime-laden
706	(it returns a handle the calling plugin needs to hold). A config
707	overlay would have nowhere to put the handle. Operators resolve
708	collisions by removing one of the registering plugins, not by
709	declaring an override.
710	
711	### [Clockworks](clockworks.md) events / standing orders
712	
713	**What fits.** The feedback-receipt path in §4 is purely Clockworks'
714	substrate — the auto-wired
715	`book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` events, the
716	standing-order `{ on, run, with? }` shape, the relay handler's
717	`(event, { params })` signature. The Reckoner does not duplicate
718	any of this; petitioners reuse the standing-order vocabulary they
719	already know.
720	
721	**What's different.** Clockworks itself does not own a registry of
722	event emitters — any apparatus may signal events into the stream,
723	constrained only by the reserved-namespace check. The petitioner
724	registry is exactly the registry Clockworks declines to maintain
725	for events: it pairs identity with authority metadata and exists
726	because petitions carry weight (priority gating, withdraw
727	authorization). Events do not need this; petitions do.
728	
729	### [Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md) trigger-types
730	
731	**What fits.** The `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source-id grammar from
732	§2 is identical to Lattice's
733	[`triggerType` grammar](apparatus/lattice.md#latticeapi-interface-provides) —
734	same prefix derivation, same kebab suffix rule, same "validated where
735	the plugin id is derivable" policy. Source-of-emission and
736	trigger-type are independent axes in both contracts.
737	
738	**What's different.** Lattice trusts the emitter to stamp
739	`pulse.source` correctly — the Lattice's D21 explicitly says "the
740	emitter stamps the source field; the Lattice trusts emitters (same
741	trust model as Stacks books' owner keys)." The petitioner contract
742	**rejects** that trust model: petitions carry authority weight
743	(`priority=immediate` is bound to `source=patron`), and a typo in an
744	emitter-stamped string would silently elevate authority. Returning a
745	handle that stamps the source internally turns the trust question
746	into a reachability question — the only path through which `emit`
747	can be called is the handle returned by a successful registration.
748	
749	---
750	
751	## 9. Open Questions
752	
753	The following follow-ups are deliberately deferred. Each names the
754	trade-off the contract consciously took, the trigger that would
755	re-open the question, and the shape the answer would likely take.
756	
757	### a. Base-class / shared-utility extraction
758	
759	- **Question.** Should there be an exported `PetitionerBase` class, a
760	  `mountPetitioner()` helper, or a shared `@shardworks/petitioner-utils`
761	  package that the third-and-beyond petitioner extends instead of
762	  hand-writing the registration call?
763	- **Trade-off.** v0 documents shared patterns as recipes only (per
764	  D11 in the commission brief). Two consumers (`patron` +
765	  `vision-keeper`) is below the threshold where the right shape for
766	  a base class becomes obvious — premature extraction would lock in
767	  the wrong abstraction and force a second migration when a third
768	  consumer's needs differ.
769	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A third real petitioner whose code
770	  duplicates a non-trivial chunk of the keeper's registration /
771	  withdraw / feedback wiring.
772	
773	### b. Lifecycle hook surface
774	
775	- **Question.** Should the registration descriptor accept
776	  `canRetry`, `onDefer`, `onAccept`, or other invocation-time hooks
777	  that run inside the Reckoner's process before / after a petition's
778	  state transitions?
779	- **Trade-off.** v0 declares no hooks (per D8 and `c-mod9a48y`).
780	  CDC + standing-order observation covers the need without an
781	  invocation-ordering contract; adding hooks pre-emptively would
782	  introduce ordering questions the framework has no precedent to
783	  lean on.
784	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A real `canRetry` use case — i.e. a
785	  petitioner whose decline / defer logic genuinely needs to run in
786	  the same transaction as the lifecycle change, not a follow-up
787	  observation pass.
788	
789	### c. Declarative kit-channel re-evaluation
790	
791	- **Question.** Should there be a `petitioners` field on the kit
792	  export so a kit-only plugin (no apparatus, no `start()`) can
793	  register a petitioner without contributing an apparatus?
794	- **Trade-off.** v0 is programmatic-only (per D1 / D13). The
795	  registration is runtime-laden — the returned handle has no
796	  natural home in a static kit declaration — and Clerk's writ-type
797	  precedent ran the same trade-off and reached the same conclusion.
798	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A third-party kit-only petitioner
799	  whose contribution is genuinely static (no need to hold the
800	  handle in apparatus state) and whose use case justifies the
801	  per-emit lookup the kit-only path would require.
802	
803	### d. Coordination with the Reckonings book commission
804	
805	- **Question.** What are the specific record fields and CDC payload
806	  shapes of the petitions book that §4's standing-order recipes
807	  filter on?
808	- **Trade-off.** This contract intentionally references *that* the
809	  petitions book exists and is CDC-attached without naming its
810	  schema. The Reckonings-book commission (`c-modeou1t`'s parallel,
811	  dispatched as the Reckonings book design track) owns the schema
812	  decisions.
813	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When the Reckonings-book commission
814	  lands, this contract's §4 recipes need a follow-up pass to
815	  cross-reference the actual `event.payload.entry.*` field names
816	  the recipes filter on, replacing the illustrative `source` /
817	  `lifecycleClassification` references with the real schema's
818	  vocabulary.
819	
820	### e. Renamed pulse-emitter as a future petitioner
821	
822	- **Question.** The current
823	  [Reckoner](apparatus/reckoner.md) (the queue-observer / pulse-
824	  emitter that watches writs and emits Lattice pulses) is on a
825	  rename-and-subsume-later track. Once renamed, will it become a
826	  built-in petitioner of this contract?
827	- **Trade-off.** v0 deliberately does not name it as a built-in
828	  class (per D10). Pulling it in now would conflate this contract
829	  with the rename track and bake assumptions about the renamed
830	  apparatus's source-id grammar before that track has chosen one.
831	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** The pulse-emitter rename track lands
832	  and the renamed apparatus's first commission needs to wire its
833	  petition emission against this contract — at which point it
834	  registers like any other third-party petitioner.
835	
836	### f. Handle-vs-trust-stamp tradeoff
837	
838	- **Question.** The contract requires that all `emit` calls go
839	  through the registration-time handle (per D3). Should there be an
840	  escape hatch for a non-handle-reachable caller — a long-lived
841	  process that loses its handle reference, a cross-process emit
842	  bridge, an external actor that needs to inject a petition without
843	  going through a registering apparatus's runtime?
844	- **Trade-off.** Handle-based stamping is the load-bearing reason
845	  the authority gate in §7 can be enforced structurally rather than
846	  by runtime trust. A trust-stamped escape hatch would re-introduce
847	  the failure mode the handle design exists to prevent — a typo in
848	  an emitter-stamped source string silently elevating authority.
849	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A concrete consumer with a non-handle-
850	  reachable call site and a clear story for how it survives the
851	  authority-gate invariant — likely an "auth-token-stamped emit"
852	  shape that re-derives the source through some other unforgeable
853	  channel, not a free-form trust-stamp.
854

file.content:
no per-petitioner intake validator, and no callback that runs on the
Reckoner's process before a petition is persisted. The upstream
conclusion (per `c-mod9a48y`) was that a hook surface is "no direct
callback mechanism in v0," and this contract makes that final.

The reasoning is twofold: the observation need is already covered by
the CDC + standing-order recipes in §4 without an additional
invocation-ordering contract, and pre-empting hooks now keeps the
petition lifecycle's observe-and-re-emit pattern uniform across every
petitioner. A hook surface that runs in the Reckoner's process would
introduce ordering questions (does `canRetry` race with the parent's
update event? does an `onDefer` throw block the defer transition?)
that the framework has no precedent to lean on.

**Deferred hook surface.** The most-likely future hooks are named
in [Open Questions](#open-questions) — `canRetry`, `onDefer`,
`onAccept` are the obvious candidates — and re-evaluation is gated on
a real consumer with a concrete need that CDC + re-emit cannot
satisfy. Adding a hook later is additive: a future
`registerPetitioner({ source, allowedPriorities, description, onDefer })`
shape does not invalidate any v0 caller.

---

## 6. Built-in petitioner classes

v0 ships with exactly **two** built-in petitioner classes. Both are
the smallest set the contract needs to be coherent and testable.

### `'patron'`

The patron's elevated petition source. Registered by the **Reckoner
itself** from its own `start()` — the Reckoner is the apparatus that
enforces the authority gate (see §7), so self-registering the one
identity that gate requires keeps the invariant local. This mirrors
the [Clerk's self-registration of `mandate`](apparatus/clerk.md#writ-type-registry):
the framework's one mandatory registry entry is contributed by the
apparatus that owns the registry, not by an external kit.

The patron source is the hard-coded exception to the
`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` grammar from §2. The bare `'patron'`
identifier is reserved; third-party plugins cannot register it
(blocked by the duplicate-source check in §1) and cannot reach the
Reckoner-issued patron handle.

How the patron *invokes* emit — the CLI surface, the MCP tool, the
`commission-post` interaction — is **not** part of this contract. A
downstream commission designs the patron-emit surface against the
internally-issued handle. This document fixes only how `'patron'` is
*registered*, not how the patron *invokes* emit.

### `'vision-keeper'`

The canonical worked example of a third-party petitioner. The
vision-keeper exercises every contract surface in this doc:

- It registers via `registerPetitioner` from its own apparatus's
  `start()` (§1).
- It declares a qualified source id `vision-keeper.snapshot` (§2).
- It calls `handle.emit(...)` with `priority: 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low'`
  drawn from the default allow-list (§7).
- It withdraws superseded petitions through `handle.withdraw(...)` (§3).
- It observes outcomes through a Channel-1 standing order on the
  petitions book filtered by its own source (§4).

Every example in this document is calibrated against the keeper.

### Future consumers — out of scope for v0

The renamed pulse-emitter (the apparatus currently documented as the
[Reckoner](apparatus/reckoner.md)'s queue-observer) is named **only
as a future consumer**, not a v0 deliverable. Its rename and
subsume-later track is a separate commission; pulling it into this
contract would conflate the two. It is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions) for completeness.

### Shared base classes / utility packages — recipes only

v0 documents shared registration patterns as **worked recipes**, not
as exported base classes or shared utility packages. There is no
`PetitionerBase` to extend, no `mountPetitioner()` helper, no
`@shardworks/petitioner-utils` package. Every petitioner writes the
five-line registration call from §1 against the Reckoner API directly.

Two consumers (`patron` + `vision-keeper`) is below the threshold
where the right shape for a base class becomes obvious — base-class
extraction is the kind of refactor a third petitioner usually
motivates. The earned-extraction stance is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions).

---

## 7. Authority / priority gating

The `priority=immediate` level is reserved for elevated authority,
and the gate that enforces this is **registration metadata**, not a
hard-coded `if (source === 'patron')` check inside the intake
validator.

Every `registerPetitioner` call carries an `allowedPriorities`
allow-list (optional in the descriptor; `undefined` resolves to the
default `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`):

```typescript
type PetitionPriority = 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';

interface RegisterPetitionerRequest {
  source:             string;
  allowedPriorities?: PetitionPriority[];
  description:        string;
}
```

The Reckoner's own `start()` registers `'patron'` with the full
allow-list:

```typescript
// inside reckoner apparatus start()
registerPetitionerInternal({
  source: 'patron',
  allowedPriorities: ['immediate', 'urgent', 'normal', 'low'],
  description: 'Elevated patron-originated petitions.',
}, 'builtin');
```

Every other registration (third-party plugins calling the public
`registerPetitioner`) **defaults to** `['urgent', 'normal', 'low']`
when the allow-list is omitted, and the Reckoner refuses any
registration that includes `'immediate'` for any source other than
the self-registered `'patron'`. The refusal is enforced at
registration time, not at first emit — a third-party plugin trying
to register `allowedPriorities: ['immediate', ...]` fails the
guild's startup with a `[reckoner] registerPetitioner: priority
"immediate" is reserved for the patron source` diagnostic.

The emit-time validator (the one that runs inside `handle.emit(...)`)
reads the allow-list from the **registry** for the handle's bound
source — there is no source-name comparison in the validator's body,
no `if (req.source === 'patron')` branch, and no special case for
the patron string. A petition arrives with a priority; the validator
asks the registry "is this priority in this source's allow-list?";
yes admits the petition, no throws fail-loud.

Two properties fall out of this design:

- **The constraint is queryable.** Operators (and the keeper-side
  diagnostic logging) can read `handle.allowedPriorities` to see
  what the petitioner is permitted to emit at, without parsing the
  validator's source code.
- **The gate generalizes to future elevated sources.** If a future
  apparatus needs to emit at `'immediate'` (e.g. a "guild-emergency-
  drain" source), the design space is "the Reckoner registers a
  second internal source with the full allow-list" — the validator
  does not change. There is no domain-specific name baked into the
  gate.

---

## 8. Existing precedents

The petitioner registration contract is not new framework shape — it
composes patterns the framework's adjacent apparatuses have already
settled. This section names *what fits* and *what's different* for
each precedent the contract draws from.

### [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md) writ-types

**What fits.** Clerk's writ-type registry is the closest live
precedent — same call shape, same seal timing, same fail-loud-on-
duplicate stance, same self-registration of the framework's one
mandatory entry. Specifically:

- `ClerkApi.registerWritType` ↔ `ReckonerApi.registerPetitioner` —
  programmatic, single-surface, called from the contributing
  apparatus's `start()`.
- `phase:started` registry seal ↔ identical seal timing here.
- "Two plugins cannot contribute the same writ type name" ↔
  identical duplicate-source policy here.
- The Clerk's self-registration of `mandate` ↔ the Reckoner's self-
  registration of `'patron'` (per §6).

**What's different.** Writ types are fully static descriptors
(`WritTypeConfig` is a pure data shape — states, classifications,
attrs, transitions). Petitioner registrations bind a runtime closure:
the returned `PetitionerHandle` carries `emit` and `withdraw` closures
that the registering plugin calls into. The writ-type registry has
no per-type runtime handle returned to the caller; the Clerk's
public surface is the same `ClerkApi.transition(id, …)` for every
type.

### [Spider](apparatus/spider.md) rig-template mappings

**What fits.** The framework-wide kit-vs-kit collision policy is the
shape this contract adopts in §1: a duplicate `registerPetitioner`
with the same source string is a hard startup error that names both
contributing plugins, identical to the rule the Spider documents for
[`rigTemplateMappings`](apparatus/spider.md#plugin-default-template-and-mapping)
and that applies framework-wide to Clerk `writTypes`, Spider
`blockTypes`, and Fabricator engines. The winner is never selected
by load order.

**What's different.** Spider's rig-template mappings have a
config-overlay tier: a `guild.json` `spider.rigTemplateMappings`
entry overrides any kit contribution silently. The petitioner
registry has **no config tier** — there is no `reckoner.petitioners`
section in `guild.json` — because the registration is runtime-laden
(it returns a handle the calling plugin needs to hold). A config
overlay would have nowhere to put the handle. Operators resolve
collisions by removing one of the registering plugins, not by
declaring an override.

### [Clockworks](clockworks.md) events / standing orders

**What fits.** The feedback-receipt path in §4 is purely Clockworks'
substrate — the auto-wired
`book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` events, the
standing-order `{ on, run, with? }` shape, the relay handler's
`(event, { params })` signature. The Reckoner does not duplicate
any of this; petitioners reuse the standing-order vocabulary they
already know.

**What's different.** Clockworks itself does not own a registry of
event emitters — any apparatus may signal events into the stream,
constrained only by the reserved-namespace check. The petitioner
registry is exactly the registry Clockworks declines to maintain
for events: it pairs identity with authority metadata and exists
because petitions carry weight (priority gating, withdraw
authorization). Events do not need this; petitions do.

### [Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md) trigger-types

**What fits.** The `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source-id grammar from
§2 is identical to Lattice's
[`triggerType` grammar](apparatus/lattice.md#latticeapi-interface-provides) —
same prefix derivation, same kebab suffix rule, same "validated where
the plugin id is derivable" policy. Source-of-emission and
trigger-type are independent axes in both contracts.

**What's different.** Lattice trusts the emitter to stamp
`pulse.source` correctly — the Lattice's D21 explicitly says "the
emitter stamps the source field; the Lattice trusts emitters (same
trust model as Stacks books' owner keys)." The petitioner contract
**rejects** that trust model: petitions carry authority weight
(`priority=immediate` is bound to `source=patron`), and a typo in an
emitter-stamped string would silently elevate authority. Returning a
handle that stamps the source internally turns the trust question
into a reachability question — the only path through which `emit`
can be called is the handle returned by a successful registration.

---

## 9. Open Questions

The following follow-ups are deliberately deferred. Each names the
trade-off the contract consciously took, the trigger that would
re-open the question, and the shape the answer would likely take.

### a. Base-class / shared-utility extraction

- **Question.** Should there be an exported `PetitionerBase` class, a
  `mountPetitioner()` helper, or a shared `@shardworks/petitioner-utils`
  package that the third-and-beyond petitioner extends instead of
  hand-writing the registration call?
- **Trade-off.** v0 documents shared patterns as recipes only (per
  D11 in the commission brief). Two consumers (`patron` +
  `vision-keeper`) is below the threshold where the right shape for
  a base class becomes obvious — premature extraction would lock in
  the wrong abstraction and force a second migration when a third
  consumer's needs differ.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A third real petitioner whose code
  duplicates a non-trivial chunk of the keeper's registration /
  withdraw / feedback wiring.

### b. Lifecycle hook surface

- **Question.** Should the registration descriptor accept
  `canRetry`, `onDefer`, `onAccept`, or other invocation-time hooks
  that run inside the Reckoner's process before / after a petition's
  state transitions?
- **Trade-off.** v0 declares no hooks (per D8 and `c-mod9a48y`).
  CDC + standing-order observation covers the need without an
  invocation-ordering contract; adding hooks pre-emptively would
  introduce ordering questions the framework has no precedent to

  petitioner whose decline / defer logic genuinely needs to run in
  the same transaction as the lifecycle change, not a follow-up

### c. Declarative kit-channel re-evaluation

- **Trade-off.** v0 is programmatic-only (per D1 / D13). The
  registration is runtime-laden — the returned handle has no

  whose contribution is genuinely static (no need to hold the
  handle in apparatus state) and whose use case justifies the
  per-emit lookup the kit-only path would require.

### d. Coordination with the Reckonings book commission

- **Question.** What are the specific record fields and CDC payload
  shapes of the petitions book that §4's standing-order recipes

  petitions book exists and is CDC-attached without naming its
  schema. The Reckonings-book commission (`c-modeou1t`'s parallel,

- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When the Reckonings-book commission
  lands, this contract's §4 recipes need a follow-up pass to
  cross-reference the actual `event.payload.entry.*` field names
  the recipes filter on, replacing the illustrative `source` /
  `lifecycleClassification` references with the real schema's
  vocabulary.

- **Question.** The current

  emitter that watches writs and emits Lattice pulses) is on a
  rename-and-subsume-later track. Once renamed, will it become a
  built-in petitioner of this contract?
- **Trade-off.** v0 deliberately does not name it as a built-in
  class (per D10). Pulling it in now would conflate this contract

  apparatus's source-id grammar before that track has chosen one.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** The pulse-emitter rename track lands
  and the renamed apparatus's first commission needs to wire its
  petition emission against this contract — at which point it
  registers like any other third-party petitioner.

### f. Handle-vs-trust-stamp tradeoff

- **Question.** The contract requires that all `emit` calls go
  through the registration-time handle (per D3). Should there be an
  escape hatch for a non-handle-reachable caller — a long-lived
  process that loses its handle reference, a cross-process emit
  bridge, an external actor that needs to inject a petition without
  going through a registering apparatus's runtime?
- **Trade-off.** Handle-based stamping is the load-bearing reason
  the authority gate in §7 can be enforced structurally rather than
  by runtime trust. A trust-stamped escape hatch would re-introduce
  the failure mode the handle design exists to prevent — a typo in
  an emitter-stamped source string silently elevating authority.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A concrete consumer with a non-handle-
  reachable call site and a clear story for how it survives the
  authority-gate invariant — likely an "auth-token-stamped emit"
  shape that re-derives the source through some other unforgeable
  channel, not a free-form trust-stamp.
