input.content:
# Petitioner Registration — Contract

Status: **Draft**

> **⚠️ v0 scope.** This document fixes the *registration-side* contract
> between the (forthcoming) petition-scheduler Reckoner and the plugins
> that emit petitions to it. v0 covers the kit-static registration
> shape, the source-id registry, the string-keyed emit and withdraw
> surface, the three opt-in feedback channels, the multi-dimensional
> priority schema, the petition → writ materialization boundary, and
> the v0 stance on lifecycle hooks (none) and shared base classes
> (none — recipes only). The Reckoner's combination function (how
> dimensions become a scheduling weight), the Reckonings book schema,
> the patron-emit surface (CLI / MCP), the renamed queue-observer /
> pulse-emitter, and any non-`patron-bridge` / non-`vision-keeper`
> petitioner are explicitly out of scope. See
> [Open Questions](#open-questions) for the named follow-ups.

---

## Dependencies

```
contract spans: ['clerk', 'clockworks', 'lattice', 'spider']
```

- **The [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md)** — the closest live precedent for
  a kit-static registry sealed at `phase:started`. The petitioner
  registry mirrors Clerk's writ-type registry shape (kit declaration,
  duplicate check, fail-loud-on-unknown stance). In addition, Clerk
  is the writ authority — the Reckoner calls `clerk.post()` to
  materialize approved petitions as writs (see §9).
- **[Clockworks](clockworks.md)** — the event-and-CDC substrate the
  feedback-receipt section routes through. Standing orders and the
  auto-wired `book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}`
  events are the canonical petitioner-feedback path in v0.
- **The [Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md)** — the source of the
  `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source-id grammar this contract adopts,
  and the precedent for the **emitter-trust** model used at emit
  time (caller stamps source, registry validates that the source is
  registered).
- **The [Spider](apparatus/spider.md)** — the source of the
  framework-wide kit-vs-kit collision policy this contract adopts:
  a duplicate kit declaration with the same source string is a hard
  startup error that names both contributing plugins.

---

## 1. Registration mechanism

A plugin declares itself a petitioner by contributing a `petitioners`
entry from its kit (or apparatus's `supportKit`):

```typescript
// In a plugin's kit export
export default {
  kit: {
    requires: ['reckoner'],
    petitioners: [
      {
        source: 'vision-keeper.snapshot',
        description:
          'Periodic vision-vs-reality snapshots emitted when ' +
          'the keeper observes drift worth surfacing.',
      },
    ],
  },
};
```

Registration is **kit-static**. The Reckoner consumes the
`petitioners` contribution type, scans every kit and apparatus
supportKit for entries, and builds the closed registry of petitioner
sources at startup. There is no programmatic `registerPetitioner(...)`
runtime call, no handle returned, and no closure-based binding.
Petitioners that need to emit look up the Reckoner's API at runtime
via `guild().apparatus<ReckonerApi>('reckoner')` and call
`reckoner.emit(...)` directly with their declared source string.

Each petitioner entry carries a small descriptor:

```typescript
interface PetitionerDescriptor {
  /** Fully-qualified source id. See §2 for the grammar. */
  source: string;
  /** Short human-facing description for diagnostics and Oculus. */
  description: string;
}
```

Note the absence of an `allowedPriorities` field. Under the multi-
dimensional priority schema (see §7) there is no per-source priority
allow-list. Petitioners declare honest dimension values at emit time;
authority emerges from the dimensions, not from a registration-time
gate.

No optional `onAccept` / `canRetry` / `onDefer` hooks are accepted in
v0. The deferred hook surface is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions); the rationale is that the upstream
conclusion (per `c-mod9a48y`) already settled "no direct callback
mechanism in v0," and the observation need is covered by the CDC +
standing-order recipes in §4 without an additional invocation-
ordering contract.

### Registry seal

The petitioner registry seals at the framework's global
`phase:started` signal — by the time every apparatus's `start()` has
finished, the registry is built, frozen, and inspectable. The seal
is **per-plugin-load-cycle** in framing: each plugin's kit
contributions are collected during its load, and once the framework
declares plugin loading complete (today: `phase:started`), no
further additions are accepted. For Nexus today (no dynamic plugin
loading) this is identical to a global seal at `phase:started`. The
framing positions the contract correctly for future dynamic plugin
loading, where new plugins joining post-startup would get their own
registration windows as they load.

After the seal, a runtime `reckoner.emit(...)` call carrying an
unregistered source fails loud:
`[reckoner] emit: source "<source>" is not registered. Registered
sources: …` — naming the offending source and listing the registered
set.

### Kit-vs-kit collision policy

Two `petitioners` entries with the same `source` string are a
**hard error**. The Reckoner refuses to start with a diagnostic that
names *both* contributing plugin ids and the conflicting source —
the winner is never selected by load order. This mirrors the
framework-wide rule the Spider documents for `rigTemplateMappings`
and that Clerk applies to writ-types, Spider to `blockTypes` /
`rigTemplateMappings`, and the Fabricator to engine designs:

> Two kits contributing a [registration entry] for the same key —
> including a plugin's own self-registered defaults — refuse to
> start the guild; the startup error names both contributing plugins
> and the conflicting key.

Silent first-or-last-wins is rejected because a typo in a source
string would silently corrupt the registry — exactly the failure
mode the fail-loud rule exists to surface.

---

## 2. Petitioner identity

The Reckoner maintains a closed registry of petitioner sources. Only
a source declared through a kit's `petitioners` declaration may emit;
an unknown source at emit time is rejected fail-loud. There is no
silent fallback.

### Source-id grammar

A source id has the form **`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`** — the
contributing plugin's derived id, a literal `.`, then a kebab-case
suffix (lowercase letters, digits, and single-hyphen separators; no
leading or trailing hyphen). The grammar matches Lattice trigger-
types and Clerk link-kinds. Examples:

- `vision-keeper.snapshot`
- `vision-keeper.drift-detected`
- `patron-bridge.commission` (illustrative — see §6)
- `coinmaster.balance-low` (illustrative future consumer)

The `{pluginId}.` prefix is validated at registration time wherever
the plugin id is derivable from the call site — kit declarations
are attributed to the contributing plugin by the framework's loader.
Malformed ids and mismatched prefixes hard-fail at startup, never at
first emit. Cross-plugin collisions are made structurally impossible
by the prefix rule and operationally impossible by the duplicate-
registration check in §1.

There are no hard-coded exceptions to the grammar. The patron is a
normal petitioner contributed by the patron-bridge apparatus (see
§6); its source is `patron-bridge.commission` (or whatever the
bridge apparatus chooses), prefixed and registered like every other
petitioner.

### Source authentication — emitter trust

Petitioners pass `source` as a string argument to
`reckoner.emit(...)`. The Reckoner trusts the emitter's claim — the
same trust model the Lattice uses for `pulse.source`. The Reckoner
validates that the source is registered; an unregistered source
fails loud.

This is explicitly **not** a structural authority proof. A typo in
an emit call site that lands on another registered source would
route the petition under that source's identity. The trade-off is
detailed in §8 (Trust model). The summary: the typo-elevation
surface is narrow under the multi-dimensional priority schema (no
per-source priority allow-lists; authority emerges from honestly-
claimed dimensions), and remaining mitigations are runtime fail-loud
on unregistered sources, code review, and a future cross-plugin
lint scanning emit-source string literals against the registered-
source set.

---

## 3. Emit-petition interface

The Reckoner exposes a string-keyed `emit` and `withdraw` surface
on its public API:

```typescript
interface ReckonerApi {
  /** Emit a petition. Returns the persisted PetitionDoc. */
  emit(request: EmitPetitionRequest): Promise<PetitionDoc>;

  /**
   * Withdraw a previously-emitted petition. Caller must declare the
   * source the petition was emitted under; Reckoner validates the
   * stored petition's source matches.
   */
  withdraw(
    petitionId: string,
    source:     string,
    reason?:    string,
  ): Promise<PetitionDoc>;
}
```

There is no `PetitionerHandle` type, no closure-based binding, and
no restriction on which apparatus may obtain the Reckoner's API —
any plugin that has access to
`guild().apparatus<ReckonerApi>('reckoner')` can call `emit`. Source
registration is the gate, not handle reachability.

### `emit` — call shape and return type

The emit call carries the petition payload. The petitioner declares
the source, the priority dimensions, the writ payload that should be
materialized on approval, and any auxiliary structured data:

```typescript
interface EmitPetitionRequest {
  /** Registered source id. See §2. */
  source: string;

  /** Multi-dimensional priority. See §7. */
  priority: Priority;

  /** Full writ payload to materialize on approval. See §9. */
  writRequest: WritRequest;

  /**
   * Opaque petitioner-defined structured data forwarded to the
   * materialized writ. See §9.
   */
  details?: unknown;

  /**
   * Additive non-priority metadata (multi-instance discrimination,
   * observability hints, diagnostic tags). See §6 for usage.
   */
  labels?: Record<string, string>;
}
```

The Reckoner generates the petition id at persist time and returns
it on the `PetitionDoc`. **The petitioner does not pre-allocate
ids.** The petition id is the petitioner's work-tracking key —
usable immediately for logging, diagnostic correlation, and future
`withdraw` calls.

`emit` is **final-on-call**. There is no draft phase, no
`emitDraft()`, and no commit step. The petition lands in the
Reckoner's `pending` state immediately and becomes visible to the
scheduler on its next tick. v0 deliberately omits the draft surface:
the petition shape is already coarse, and adding a draft state would
duplicate the
[Clerk's `new` phase](apparatus/clerk.md#mandates-lifecycle-an-example-registered-type)
without a named consumer to motivate the second-write step.
Petitioners that need to assemble data across multiple steps assemble
it before calling `emit`. Adding drafts later is purely additive and
is named in [Open Questions](#open-questions) as a deferred
consideration.

The emit return type is the **full persisted `PetitionDoc`** — same
shape the Reckoner stores in the petitions book, including its
generated id, timestamps, lifecycle state, and the Reckoner-stamped
fields the petitioner did not supply. This mirrors `ClerkApi.post`
and `LatticeApi.emit`: both return the complete persisted document
so the caller can chain reads, log the resulting id, or pin
observability without a second lookup.

### `withdraw` — petitioner-initiated

A petitioner withdraws a previously-emitted petition by passing the
petition id, the source it was emitted under, and an optional
reason:

```typescript
await reckoner.withdraw(
  petitionId,
  'vision-keeper.snapshot',
  'Snapshot superseded by drift detected before this one ran.',
);
```

The withdraw call's contract:

- **Source check.** The Reckoner asserts the stored petition's
  `source` matches the supplied source. A petitioner trying to
  withdraw another petitioner's petition (by passing the wrong
  source) throws fail-loud.
- **Allowed only from non-terminal states.** A petition can be
  withdrawn while it is in `new`, `pending`, or `deferred`.
  Petitions in any terminal state — already accepted, declined,
  executed, or withdrawn — reject the call. The terminal-state
  list is the petition lifecycle's own (per `c-modaqnpt`); withdraw
  lands the petition in the lifecycle's petitioner-initiated
  `withdrawn` terminal.
- **Optional reason.** A short freeform string surfaced on the
  resulting decline-feedback channel and persisted on the petition
  document. Omitting it is allowed; passing one helps the standing-
  order recipes in §4 explain themselves to the patron.
- **Returns the updated `PetitionDoc`.** Same shape as `emit`'s
  return — the post-transition snapshot, useful for logging the
  resolved-at timestamp without a second read.
- **Cancels the materialized writ if approval has already
  occurred.** When a withdraw is processed on an approved petition
  that has already produced a writ, the Reckoner additionally
  transitions the writ to its type's `cancelled` terminal state via
  Clerk's transition API. Withdrawing an in-flight writ is a more
  invasive operation than withdrawing a not-yet-approved petition;
  petitioners should prefer withdrawing early.

There is no Reckoner-initiated withdraw call exposed on the public
API. The Reckoner's own decline / defer transitions are observed
through the feedback recipes in §4; they are not invoked by the
petitioner.

---

## 4. Feedback receipt patterns

A petitioner observes what happened to its petitions through
**[Clockworks](clockworks.md) standing orders** keyed on the auto-
wired CDC events the Reckonings book emits. There is no in-process
subscribe surface on the Reckoner — the v0 conclusion (per
`c-mod9a54n`) is that a parallel subscribe path would duplicate the
substrate that already exists, lack durability and replay across
daemon restarts, and add an invocation-ordering contract the
framework has not yet earned. Standing orders are durable (the
trigger lives in `guild.json`), survive process restarts, and slot
into the same event-routing fabric every other apparatus uses.

The Reckonings book is owned by the Reckoner and its CDC events are
auto-wired by [Clockworks](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
through the standard
`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` pattern — the
same substrate every other plugin's books ride on. (The book exists
and is CDC-attached; its specific record fields and payload schema
are owned by the parallel Reckonings-book commission, per
`c-modeou1t`. This contract relies only on the existence of CDC
events keyed by the petition's `source`, not on any specific schema
field.)

A petitioner picks one of three opt-in feedback channels.

### Channel 1 — event-driven standing order (the canonical path)

The recommended channel. The petitioner declares a standing order in
`guild.json` keyed on the petitions book's `updated` event, filtered
to its own source via the standard standing-order filter the order
hands to its relay through `with:`. The relay reads the post-commit
event payload, decides whether the lifecycle change is one the
petitioner cares about, and re-emits / cleans up / notifies as
appropriate.

The vision-keeper's decline-watch recipe:

```jsonc
{
  "clockworks": {
    "standingOrders": [
      {
        "on": "book.reckoner.petitions.updated",
        "run": "vision-keeper-on-decline",
        "with": {
          "filterSource":         "vision-keeper.snapshot",
          "filterTerminalAttr":   "declined"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The relay's handler (a vision-keeper-side relay contributed via the
plugin's [`ClockworksKit`](clockworks.md#clockworkskit)) inspects
the event payload at handler time:

```typescript
// vision-keeper/src/relays/on-decline.ts
import { relay } from '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus';

export default relay({
  name: 'vision-keeper-on-decline',
  handler: async (event, { params }) => {
    const entry = event?.payload?.entry as { source?: string };
    if (!entry || entry.source !== params.filterSource) return;
    // Inspect the entry's lifecycle attrs / decline reason and
    // re-emit the snapshot petition with adjusted context, log to
    // the keeper's own book, ping a Lattice channel — whatever the
    // keeper does on a decline. The Reckoner API is reachable via
    // guild().apparatus<ReckonerApi>('reckoner') for re-emission.
  },
});
```

Two things worth highlighting. First, the filter on
`event.payload.entry.source` is the load-bearing one — every
petition-book CDC event carries the entry as its payload, and the
source field is what scopes the keeper's relay to its own petitions
without coupling to other plugins' sources. Second, the standing
order points at a relay the keeper itself ships in its
`supportKit.relays`; standing orders never name the petitioner
plugin directly.

The `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` event name is the auto-wired
form (`book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.updated`) — the petitioner does not
need to declare the event in `clockworks.events`; it is supplied by
the Stacks-Clockworks bridge automatically.

### Channel 2 — polling the petitions book

For petitioners that need point-in-time aggregate state (a status
dashboard, a periodic reconciliation pass, a quick "do I have
anything outstanding?" check), the channel is a direct read of the
Reckonings book filtered by the petitioner's own source:

```typescript
// In a vision-keeper-owned tool or scheduled relay handler:
const stacks  = guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
const book    = stacks.book<PetitionDoc>('reckoner', 'petitions');
const mine    = await book.list({
  where: [['source', '=', 'vision-keeper.snapshot']],
});
const pending = mine.filter((p) => p.lifecycleClassification !== 'terminal');
```

Polling is appropriate when the petitioner wants a snapshot rather
than a stream — daily reports, reconciliation, ad-hoc inspection.
For event-by-event reaction, prefer Channel 1 — polling cannot
distinguish a transition from steady state without keeping its own
side-channel state.

### Channel 3 — fire-and-forget

The third channel is no channel at all: the petitioner emits and
walks away. Acceptance, decline, defer, or successful execution all
happen without the petitioner ever observing the outcome. Re-emission
is driven by the petitioner's own conditions — when the same
condition triggers again, the petitioner emits again; the Reckoner's
lifecycle (per `c-modaqnpt`) handles dedupe / supersede on its own.

Fire-and-forget is the right channel for petitioners whose intent is
"I noticed *X* happened; route this to the scheduler if it matters."
A `vision-keeper.snapshot` triggered hourly by a cron-like relay can
emit without subscribing — if it is declined as a duplicate of an
in-flight snapshot petition, the next hour's tick will emit again
when the keeper's conditions re-evaluate. The petitioner never has
to encode lifecycle reactions; the cadence handles it.

### Channel selection — recommended order

| Need | Channel |
|------|---------|
| React to declines / defers / completions in real time | **1 — standing order** |
| Periodic reconciliation, dashboards, "what's outstanding?" | **2 — polling** |
| "I noticed *X*; route it" with no follow-up | **3 — fire-and-forget** |

A petitioner is free to combine channels (a dashboard tool polling
+ a relay reacting to declines) — they observe the same underlying
book through the same auto-wired CDC substrate. The recipes do not
compete; they slice the same data different ways.

---

## 5. Lifecycle hooks

**v0 declares no lifecycle hooks.** A petitioner's full declarative
surface is the two-field `PetitionerDescriptor` shape from §1
(`source`, `description`). There is no `onAccept`, no `canRetry`,
no `onDefer`, no `onWithdraw`, no per-petitioner intake validator,
and no callback that runs in the Reckoner's process before a
petition is persisted. The upstream conclusion (per `c-mod9a48y`)
was that a hook surface is "no direct callback mechanism in v0,"
and this contract makes that final.

The reasoning is twofold: the observation need is already covered
by the CDC + standing-order recipes in §4 without an additional
invocation-ordering contract, and pre-empting hooks now keeps the
petition lifecycle's observe-and-re-emit pattern uniform across
every petitioner. A hook surface that runs in the Reckoner's
process would introduce ordering questions (does `canRetry` race
with the parent's update event? does an `onDefer` throw block the
defer transition?) that the framework has no precedent to lean on.

**Deferred hook surface.** The most-likely future hooks are named
in [Open Questions](#open-questions) — `canRetry`, `onDefer`,
`onAccept` are the obvious candidates — and re-evaluation is gated
on a real consumer with a concrete need that CDC + re-emit cannot
satisfy. Adding a hook later is additive: a future
`PetitionerDescriptor` shape that includes `onDefer` does not
invalidate any v0 caller.

---

## 6. Built-in petitioner classes

v0 ships with exactly **one** built-in petitioner class for the
contract's worked example. There is no patron special-case in this
contract — the patron's emit path is owned by a separate apparatus
that registers normally (see "Patron-bridge — out of contract"
below).

### `vision-keeper`

The canonical worked example of a third-party petitioner. The
vision-keeper exercises every contract surface in this doc:

- It declares its petitioner source statically in its kit (§1).
- It uses a qualified source id `vision-keeper.snapshot` (§2).
- It calls `reckoner.emit(...)` with the structured priority
  dimensions drawn from §7 — most often
  `{ visionRelation: 'vision-violator', severity: 'serious',
     scope: 'major-area', time: { decay: true, deadline: null },
     domain: ['quality'] }`
  for drift-detected snapshots, or
  `{ visionRelation: 'vision-advancer', severity: 'moderate',
     scope: 'minor-area', time: { decay: false, deadline: null },
     domain: ['feature'] }`
  for proactive elaboration nudges.
- It includes the structured snapshot data in `details` so the rig
  that processes the resulting writ has the full snapshot available
  without joining to overlay books.
- It uses `labels` to discriminate per-vision instances when
  multiple visions are tracked:
  `labels: { 'vision-keeper.io/vision-id': 'nexus' }`. The source
  remains singleton (`vision-keeper.snapshot`) regardless of
  vision count.
- It withdraws superseded petitions through
  `reckoner.withdraw(...)` (§3).
- It observes outcomes through a Channel-1 standing order on the
  petitions book filtered by its own source (§4).

Every example in this document is calibrated against the keeper.

### Patron-bridge — out of contract

The patron's emit path (CLI surface, MCP tool, `commission-post`
interaction) is owned by a separate **patron-bridge** apparatus,
which registers as a normal petitioner under a normal
`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source (e.g. `patron-bridge.commission`).
The patron-bridge apparatus is not specified by this contract — its
surface design is a downstream commission. From the Reckoner's
perspective, the patron-bridge is just another petitioner; its
petitions claim whatever dimensions the patron's input warrants,
and the Reckoner schedules them by the same rules as every other
petition.

There is no hard-coded `'patron'` source name, no Reckoner-self-
registered patron, and no per-source priority allow-list reserving
high authority for patron-originated petitions. The patron's
authority emerges from the dimensions its petitions honestly claim —
typically `severity: 'critical'` and concrete `visionRelation` /
`domain` values that combine into high scheduling weight.

### Future consumers — out of scope for v0

The renamed pulse-emitter (the apparatus currently documented as
the [Reckoner](apparatus/reckoner.md)'s queue-observer) is named
**only as a future consumer**, not a v0 deliverable. Its rename
and subsume-later track is a separate commission; pulling it into
this contract would conflate the two. It is named in
[Open Questions](#open-questions) for completeness.

### Shared base classes / utility packages — recipes only

v0 documents shared registration patterns as **worked recipes**,
not as exported base classes or shared utility packages. There is
no `PetitionerBase` to extend, no `mountPetitioner()` helper, no
`@shardworks/petitioner-utils` package. Every petitioner writes
the small kit declaration from §1 against the Reckoner contract
directly.

A single first-party consumer (`vision-keeper`) plus the patron-
bridge is below the threshold where the right shape for a base
class becomes obvious — base-class extraction is the kind of
refactor a third petitioner usually motivates. The earned-
extraction stance is named in [Open Questions](#open-questions).

---

## 7. Priority dimensions

The petition's `priority` field is a structured object across six
dimensions. Designed to be **inspectable and defensible** rather
than mechanically objective — petitioners declare honest inputs,
the Reckoner does judgment-laden contextual collapse. The shared
vocabulary makes disagreements visible and resolvable in review,
which is the real win over the original 4-tier
`immediate / urgent / normal / low` enum.

```typescript
type Priority = {
  visionRelation:
    | 'vision-blocker'
    | 'vision-violator'
    | 'vision-advancer'
    | 'vision-neutral';
  severity:
    | 'critical' | 'serious' | 'moderate' | 'minor';
  scope:
    | 'whole-product' | 'major-area' | 'minor-area';
  time: {
    decay:    boolean;
    deadline: string | null;        // ISO date or null
  };
  domain: Array<
    | 'security' | 'compliance' | 'cost' | 'feature' | 'quality'
    | 'infrastructure' | 'documentation' | 'research' | 'ergonomics'
  >;
  complexity?:
    | 'mechanical' | 'bounded' | 'exploratory' | 'open-ended';
};
```

### `visionRelation`

How does this petition relate to the product vision? The values
name a **relationship type**, not a magnitude — magnitude lives on
`severity`.

- `vision-blocker` — the vision is unreachable without this; future-
  blocking, even if everything works fine today (e.g. "we can't
  reach the multi-product vision until plural-petitioner allocation
  exists").
- `vision-violator` — the current product state actively diverges
  from the vision; present-tense degradation (e.g. "the seal step
  regularly drops commits and that contradicts the reliability
  vision").
- `vision-advancer` — pushes toward an aspirational, not-yet-realized
  aspect of the vision (e.g. "add the proposed Vision-Keeper
  apparatus").
- `vision-neutral` — doesn't touch the product vision; operational,
  hygiene, internal tooling, research instrumentation.

The split between `blocker` and `violator` matters: a blocker is
about the *future*, a violator is about the *present*. They warrant
different scheduling treatment (a present violation is a live
wound; a future block can sometimes be deferred until adjacent work
catches up).

### `severity`

If this petition is not acted on, how bad is the situation? Pure
magnitude axis, independent of vision-relation.

- `critical` — production breakage, security incident, irreversible
  damage accruing, agents fully blocked.
- `serious` — significant ongoing degradation; humans or agents
  working around it daily; cost or risk accumulating.
- `moderate` — noticeable but tolerable; works around itself most
  days.
- `minor` — easy to live with; cosmetic, convenience, polish.

Vision-neutral items still take a severity. A critical operational
issue (e.g. "the daemon crashes every hour") has
`visionRelation: 'vision-neutral', severity: 'critical'`. The
Reckoner should still prioritize it.

For `vision-advancer` petitions, severity reads as the value /
eagerness of the advancement rather than damage from inaction. The
same scale orders correctly (critical = "we really want this";
minor = "nice-to-have someday"); the interpretation shifts to fit
the relationship type.

### `scope`

What fraction of the system is affected?

- `whole-product` — every user / every commission / every guild.
- `major-area` — a major feature or subsystem.
- `minor-area` — a small slice, one workflow, one command.

### `time`

Time pressure has two genuinely independent axes; collapsing them
into one enum forced petitioners to pick a primary flavor for items
that have both.

- `decay: true` — drift sentinels, security exposure, accumulating
  technical debt, anything where waiting compounds the problem.
- `deadline: <iso-date>` — pledged demos, external commitments,
  calendar-bound obligations.
- both — common, e.g. "we promised the demo on May 15, AND every
  day it's broken costs us users."
- neither — defer-friendly work.

### `domain`

Multi-valued tag set for orthogonal classification. **Not** a
priority axis — it tells the consumer what *kind* of work this is,
not how much it should weigh. Multi-valued because real items
routinely span tags (a "security feature" is
`['security', 'feature']`; a docs improvement to a research
instrument is `['documentation', 'research']`).

Empty array is allowed but discouraged — at least one tag should
usually apply.

The Reckoner uses domain for:

- filtering ("show me all security petitions").
- patron-tunable weighting (e.g. weight `security` higher than
  `cost`).
- reporting ("this week's docket was 60% infrastructure").

### `complexity` (optional)

Petitioner-side coarse estimate of expected agent token cost.
Refined by the Astrolabe at plan time; this exists only for early-
stage trade-offs before plans exist.

- `mechanical` — clear path, minimal exploration. Rename a field,
  add a known-shape method, regenerate from a template. Roughly
  ~50K–200K tokens.
- `bounded` — clear scope but some exploration needed. Add a
  feature whose shape is understood, fix a bug whose location is
  known, refactor within one subsystem. Roughly ~200K–1M tokens.
- `exploratory` — design judgment required; multiple viable paths;
  cross-cutting investigation. Diagnose a flaky issue, design a new
  component, refactor across subsystem boundaries. Roughly
  ~1M–5M tokens.
- `open-ended` — research, prototyping, or scope likely to grow
  under contact with reality. Multi-session work expected.
  ~5M+ tokens.
- omitted — petitioner has no basis to claim.

The token ranges are **calibration hints, not contractual**. They
will drift as agent capability changes; periodic re-tuning against
actual commission-log data is expected. Petitioners are encouraged
to omit rather than guess — omission is honest; a wild guess is
misleading.

### Defaults when omitted

The most-conservative claim — omission means "I have no strong
claim."

```typescript
{
  visionRelation: 'vision-neutral',
  severity:       'minor',
  scope:          'minor-area',
  time:           { decay: false, deadline: null },
  domain:         [],
  complexity:     undefined,
}
```

A petition with all-defaults reads as "I noticed this; do it
whenever convenient" — honest, low-claim, won't accidentally jump
the queue.

### Combination semantics

The Reckoner does **judgment-laden** collapse — these dimensions
are inputs to a contextual scheduling decision, not coordinates in
a priority space with a fixed lexicographic order. The structure
exists to make the decision *defensible and inspectable*, not to
mechanically produce an order.

Two petitioners landing on slightly different vectors for the same
item may still produce the same Reckoner verdict; that's the kind
of agreement that actually matters at the scheduling layer. The
shared vocabulary makes their disagreement visible and resolvable
in review, which is the real win over the old enum.

For deterministic fallback ordering when the Reckoner isn't
running (manual triage, incident response, dry-run inspection),
suggested precedence:

1. `severity: 'critical'` ahead of everything.
2. `visionRelation: 'vision-blocker'` next.
3. Then anything with `time.decay: true` or imminent `time.deadline`.
4. Then by severity within remaining.
5. Ties broken by scope (broader first).
6. Then by `complexity` (smaller first — quick wins flush before
   slogs).

This precedence is **suggestion, not contract** — the Reckoner is
free to weigh differently, and patrons can tune. The contract only
commits to the dimension schema.

The combination-function shape (rules / weighted sum / LLM scorer)
belongs to the Reckoner-core or Reckonings-book commission, not to
this contract.

### Deliberate omissions

Concerns considered and explicitly not added:

- **Reversibility** — one-way-door vs two-way-door is a real
  concern, but it's better captured at plan-review time than at
  petition-emit time (petitioners often don't know).
- **Dependency / unblock-count** — whether completing this
  petition unblocks others is a graph property the Reckoner can
  compute from the petition graph itself, not something the
  petitioner should self-claim.
- **A unified "urgency" scalar** — deliberately not added. The
  whole point is to avoid the lossy collapse of the original
  4-tier enum.

### No allow-list concept

There is no per-source priority allow-list. Petitioners declare
honest dimension values; mis-claiming is code-review-detectable,
parallel to the source-trust model in §2. Authority for the patron-
bridge or future security-emergency apparatuses emerges from
honestly-claimed dimensions (e.g.
`severity: 'critical', visionRelation: 'vision-violator',
domain: ['security']`), not from a privileged source name or per-
source allow-list.

---

## 8. Trust model

Petitioners stamp `source` on emit; the Reckoner trusts the
emitter's claim and validates only that the source is registered.
This is a deliberate **emitter-trust** model, identical to the
Lattice's `pulse.source` trust philosophy.

Trust is acceptable here because the typo-elevation attack surface
is narrow:

- Typo to an unregistered source name → fails loud. Caught
  instantly.
- Typo to another registered source → bug, but only an authority
  elevation in the unusual case where the typo'd source's
  honestly-claimed dimensions imply higher scheduling weight than
  the typist's intended source's. With the multi-dimensional
  priority schema (§7) there is no per-source allow-list — every
  source can claim any dimension values, so typo-elevation requires
  the typist to *also* mis-claim dimensions. The typo alone doesn't
  elevate; the typo plus dishonest dimension claims does.
- For a typo-elevation to land at runtime, the typo'd source string
  has to appear consistently in both the kit declaration and emit
  call (a mismatch between the two yields an unregistered source
  and fails loud).

That's a "narrow-but-possible" surface, on par with typo bugs
accepted elsewhere in the codebase.

### Why not handle-based or framework-mediated identity

Two alternatives were considered and rejected:

- **Handle-based authority** (closures stamp source). An earlier
  draft of this contract returned a `PetitionerHandle` from a
  programmatic registration call; the handle's `emit` and
  `withdraw` methods closed over the source string and stamped it
  internally, providing structural proof of source ownership.
  Rejected because the multi-dimensional priority schema (§7)
  eliminated per-source authority gates entirely — without an
  authority gate, the handle's structural proof was over-engineering
  for a failure mode the contract no longer surfaces.
- **Framework-mediated identity** (caller pluginId stamped via
  AsyncLocalStorage proxy at the framework level). Rejected because
  plugin authors routinely use raw timer / signal / HTTP / IO
  callbacks that drop ALS context (found in 4+ current plugins:
  Animator, Clockworks daemon, Spider, Oculus). Framework-mediated
  identity would either fail-loud (breaking any plugin that does
  deferred work) or fail-silent (silent authority drift — exactly
  the failure mode it would be meant to prevent). Detection
  mechanisms (lint, runtime warnings, framework-alternatives-
  everywhere) are non-airtight. The identity-proxy work continues
  as an independent track (`c-mofxdlwb`) for Lattice's pulse-source
  trust retirement and Stacks book-ownership enforcement, but is
  not load-bearing for petitioner authority validation.

### Mitigations

- **Runtime fail-loud** on unregistered source.
- **Code review** by humans and reviewing agents.
- **Future cross-plugin lint** scanning emit-source string literals
  against the registered-source set, warning when a literal in
  plugin A's source matches a source registered by plugin B. Kit
  declarations are static and inspectable, so the lint is feasible.

---

## 9. Petition → writ materialization

The Reckoner is a **scheduler, not an executor**. Petitions are
intent records; the work itself is performed by writs of various
types dispatched through the framework's normal Spider /
Fabricator machinery. The Reckoner's role at materialization is
purely:

- decide whether the petition is approved, deferred, declined, or
  withdrawn (the scheduling decision);
- on approval, call `clerk.post(...)` with the petition's writ
  payload to materialize the writ;
- forward the petition's `details` to the resulting writ.

The Reckoner builds no bespoke decomposition or LLM-running
infrastructure. Once the writ exists, the Reckoner's involvement
ends — Spider's rig-template logic, the Fabricator's engine
surface, and Clerk's lifecycle take over.

### `WritRequest` — the writ payload on emit

The petition carries the full writ payload to be materialized on
approval:

```typescript
interface WritRequest {
  /** Writ type — must be a registered Clerk writ type. */
  type: string;
  /** Short human-readable title describing the work. */
  title: string;
  /** Detail text. */
  body: string;
  /** Target codex name. Required. */
  codex: string;
  /**
   * Optional parent writ id. The materialized writ becomes a
   * child of this writ in the Clerk's writ tree.
   */
  parentId?: string;
}
```

Notes:

- **`codex` is required.** Aligned with the upcoming Clerk-side
  change making `codex` required on every writ at the framework
  level.
- **No `petitionId` field on `WritRequest`.** The Reckoner stamps
  the originating petition id on the writ at `clerk.post()` time
  (`writ.petitionId`); the petitioner does not supply it.

### `details` — opaque petitioner-supplied data

`emit({..., details})` accepts an opaque structured payload. The
Reckoner stores `details` on the petition record (in the petitions
book) but does not introspect it. On approval, `details` is
forwarded to the materialized writ where the dispatched rig's
implementation engine can read it.

`details` is the escape hatch for cases where a petitioner needs
to attach structured data that the rig will consume — full
snapshot payloads, configuration parameters, parsed input —
without forcing the structured data into the writ's `body` text or
requiring the petitioner to manage a separate overlay book and
cross-reference.

Most rigs probably won't read `details` at all — `title` and
`body` suffice for human-readable work descriptions and for rigs
whose behavior is fully determined by the writ's type and
lifecycle.

### Materialization flow

```
petitioner.emit({ source, priority, writRequest, details, labels })
  ↓
[Reckoner] persist petition (state: pending)
  ↓
[Reckoner] schedule — combination function over priority dimensions
  ↓
[Reckoner] approve (state: approved)
  ↓
[Reckoner] clerk.post({ ...writRequest, petitionId, details })
  ↓
[Clerk] writ created (with writ.petitionId set, writ.details forwarded)
  ↓
[Spider] rig dispatch — normal path
  ↓
[engine] reads writ.details if needed; runs work
```

### Withdrawal after approval

Withdrawing a petition that has already been approved (and
therefore already produced a writ) is allowed but more invasive
than withdrawing pre-approval. The Reckoner additionally
transitions the materialized writ to its type's `cancelled`
terminal state via Clerk's transition API. Petitioners should
prefer withdrawing early; late withdrawal incurs the cost of
unwinding in-flight work.

### Out of scope for this contract

The Reckonings book schema (specific record fields, lifecycle
phases, evaluation log shape) is owned by the parallel
Reckonings-book commission. The Clerk-side schema changes that
this section assumes — adding `WritDoc.petitionId?` and
`WritDoc.details?`, making `codex` required — belong to the
Clerk-side change set, not this contract. This contract commits
to "petition carries the writ payload; Reckoner posts on
approval, stamping petitionId on the resulting writ" — the
precise field shape will track Clerk's evolution.

---

## 10. Existing precedents

The petitioner registration contract is not new framework shape —
it composes patterns the framework's adjacent apparatuses have
already settled. This section names *what fits* and *what's
different* for each precedent the contract draws from.

### [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md) writ-types

**What fits.** Clerk's writ-type registry is the closest live
precedent — kit-static declaration, sealed at `phase:started`,
fail-loud-on-duplicate, fail-loud-on-unknown.

- `WritTypeConfig` kit contributions ↔ `petitioners` kit
  contributions — both static descriptors collected at startup.
- `phase:started` registry seal ↔ identical seal timing here.
- "Two plugins cannot contribute the same writ type name" ↔
  identical duplicate-source policy here.
- `clerk.post(...)` and `clerk.transition(...)` are Clerk's
  string-keyed runtime API ↔ `reckoner.emit(...)` and
  `reckoner.withdraw(...)` are this contract's string-keyed
  runtime API. No type-specific or source-specific handles in
  either case.

**What's different.** Clerk's writ-type registry is type-only —
there is no per-type metadata about authority. The petitioner
registry carries identity and description but no per-source
authority gate (authority emerges from petition dimensions; see
§7). At materialization time the Reckoner is a *consumer* of
Clerk — `clerk.post()` is called by the Reckoner on approval —
making this contract dependent on Clerk's writ-creation API in a
way the writ-type registry itself isn't dependent on any other
apparatus.

### [Spider](apparatus/spider.md) rig-template mappings

**What fits.** The framework-wide kit-vs-kit collision policy is
the shape this contract adopts in §1: a duplicate `petitioners`
entry with the same source string is a hard startup error that
names both contributing plugins, identical to the rule the Spider
documents for
[`rigTemplateMappings`](apparatus/spider.md#plugin-default-template-and-mapping)
and that applies framework-wide to Clerk `writTypes`, Spider
`blockTypes`, and Fabricator engines. The winner is never selected
by load order.

**What's different.** Spider's rig-template mappings have a
config-overlay tier: a `guild.json` `spider.rigTemplateMappings`
entry overrides any kit contribution silently. The petitioner
registry has **no config tier** — there is no
`reckoner.petitioners` section in `guild.json` — because there is
no operational use case for overriding a kit's petitioner
declaration without removing the kit entirely. Operators resolve
collisions by removing one of the registering plugins, not by
declaring an override.

### [Clockworks](clockworks.md) events / standing orders

**What fits.** The feedback-receipt path in §4 is purely
Clockworks' substrate — the auto-wired
`book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted}` events, the
standing-order `{ on, run, with? }` shape, the relay handler's
`(event, { params })` signature. The Reckoner does not duplicate
any of this; petitioners reuse the standing-order vocabulary they
already know.

**What's different.** Clockworks itself does not own a registry of
event emitters — any apparatus may signal events into the stream,
constrained only by the reserved-namespace check. The petitioner
registry is exactly the registry Clockworks declines to maintain
for events: it pairs identity with per-source descriptive metadata
and exists because petitions have a structured intake surface that
events do not.

### [Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md) trigger-types

**What fits.** The `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` source-id grammar
from §2 is identical to Lattice's
[`triggerType` grammar](apparatus/lattice.md#latticeapi-interface-provides) —
same prefix derivation, same kebab suffix rule, same "validated
where the plugin id is derivable" policy.

The trust model (§8) is also Lattice-aligned: caller stamps
source, substrate trusts and validates registry membership.
Lattice's D21 explicitly says "the emitter stamps the source
field; the Lattice trusts emitters" — this contract's trust model
is the same shape. Earlier drafts diverged from Lattice on this
axis; the multi-dimensional priority schema (§7) eliminated the
authority-gate that motivated divergence, so the contracts now
match.

**What's different.** Lattice has no concept of priority or
scheduling — pulses fire and are routed; there is no decision
about *whether* a pulse is dispatched. This contract adds the
priority-scheduling layer, which Lattice doesn't need.

---

## 11. Open Questions

The following follow-ups are deliberately deferred. Each names
the trade-off the contract consciously took, the trigger that
would re-open the question, and the shape the answer would
likely take.

### a. Base-class / shared-utility extraction

- **Question.** Should there be an exported `PetitionerBase`
  class, a `mountPetitioner()` helper, or a shared
  `@shardworks/petitioner-utils` package that the third-and-
  beyond petitioner extends instead of hand-writing the
  registration call?
- **Trade-off.** v0 documents shared patterns as recipes only.
  A single first-party consumer (`vision-keeper`) plus the
  patron-bridge is below the threshold where the right shape
  for a base class becomes obvious — premature extraction would
  lock in the wrong abstraction.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A third real petitioner whose code
  duplicates a non-trivial chunk of the keeper's emit /
  withdraw / feedback wiring.

### b. Lifecycle hook surface

- **Question.** Should the registration descriptor accept
  `canRetry`, `onDefer`, `onAccept`, or other invocation-time
  hooks that run inside the Reckoner's process before / after a
  petition's state transitions?
- **Trade-off.** v0 declares no hooks. CDC + standing-order
  observation covers the need without an invocation-ordering
  contract; adding hooks pre-emptively would introduce ordering
  questions the framework has no precedent to lean on.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A real `canRetry` use case — a
  petitioner whose decline / defer logic genuinely needs to
  run in the same transaction as the lifecycle change, not a
  follow-up observation pass.

### c. Coordination with the Reckonings book commission

- **Question.** What are the specific record fields and CDC
  payload shapes of the petitions book that §4's standing-
  order recipes filter on?
- **Trade-off.** This contract intentionally references *that*
  the petitions book exists and is CDC-attached without naming
  its schema. The Reckonings-book commission owns the schema
  decisions.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When the Reckonings-book
  commission lands, this contract's §4 recipes need a follow-
  up pass to cross-reference the actual `event.payload.entry.*`
  field names the recipes filter on, replacing the illustrative
  `source` / `lifecycleClassification` references with the real
  schema's vocabulary.

### d. Clerk schema changes for petition→writ materialization

- **Question.** What is the exact Clerk schema for the fields §9
  references — `WritDoc.petitionId?`, `WritDoc.details?`, and
  `codex` becoming required on every writ?
- **Trade-off.** This contract commits to "petition carries the
  writ payload; Reckoner posts on approval, stamping petitionId
  on the resulting writ" — the precise Clerk-side field shape is
  owned by the Clerk-side change track, not this contract. Where
  exactly `details` lives on the writ (a first-class field, in
  the per-plugin `status` overlay, or fetched from the petition
  record via `writ.petitionId`) is a Clerk schema decision.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When the Clerk-side change set
  lands, this contract's §9 needs a follow-up pass to name the
  actual field shapes.

### e. Renamed pulse-emitter as a future petitioner

- **Question.** The current
  [Reckoner](apparatus/reckoner.md) (the queue-observer / pulse-
  emitter that watches writs and emits Lattice pulses) is on a
  rename-and-subsume-later track. Once renamed, will it become
  a built-in petitioner of this contract?
- **Trade-off.** v0 deliberately does not name it as a built-in
  class. Pulling it in now would conflate this contract with
  the rename track and bake assumptions about the renamed
  apparatus's source-id grammar before that track has chosen
  one.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** The pulse-emitter rename track
  lands and the renamed apparatus's first commission needs to
  wire its petition emission against this contract — at which
  point it registers like any other third-party petitioner.

### f. Combination-function design

- **Question.** How does the Reckoner combine the six priority
  dimensions into a single scheduling weight? Rules, weighted
  sum, LLM scorer?
- **Trade-off.** This contract commits only to the dimension
  schema, not to the combination function. The function's
  design belongs to the Reckoner-core or Reckonings-book
  commission.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When a Reckoner-core scheduling
  prototype lands and reveals what the function shape actually
  is, this contract may want to reference it (or remain
  agnostic permanently).

### g. Calibration loop for `complexity` claims

- **Question.** How do petitioner `complexity` claims get
  calibrated against actual token spend? Tracked separately in
  `c-mohd0luw`.
- **Trade-off.** The token-range hints in §7 are explicitly not
  contractual — they will drift as agent capability changes.
  Calibration is an operational concern outside this contract.
- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Calibration data accumulating to
  the point where a structural mechanism (calibrated by-
  petitioner bias offsets, e.g.) would help.
